Tafseer:
Completion of the theme of the previous Surah:
This Surah begins without any Bismillah (formula) or introduction. The reason for this, as pointed out earlier, is that it is in fact the completion of the theme of warning (Inzar) with which the previous Surah ended. The final verses of the previous Surah and the initial verses of this Surah have formed a connecting link. In the commentary of Surah Al-Bara'at (At-Tawbah), we have pointed out some of the merits of this type of connection.

"Linnas" (for the people) refers to the polytheists of Makkah:
By "Linnas" (the people), as narrated from Hazrat Ibn Abbas, the polytheists of Makkah are meant. Since these people persisted in turning away from Allah's reminder, Allah also turned away from them and mentioned them with a general term. The style of expression reflects a kind of aversion, dislike, and sorrow, and several examples of this have passed before.

Reminder of a Divine Sunnah (Way):
The time of reckoning for the people has drawn very near. This is not merely a threat but a statement of fact. We have explained in this book that when Allah sends His Messenger to a nation, only one path remains for them: to believe. If they do not choose this path, they are inevitably destroyed after the completion of proof (Itmam al-Hujjah). In the light of this Divine Sunnah, it is stated that after the advent and call of the Messenger, the Day of Reckoning for these polytheists has drawn very near, yet they remain in heedlessness, turning away from the Messenger's reminders and warnings. Here, two of their states are mentioned simultaneously: heedlessness and turning away. Heedlessness—meaning indifference to the core realities of life—is in itself proof of human wretchedness and a major transgression. However, this crime becomes far more severe when a servant of Allah exerts all his effort to shake and awaken them, yet the people are so overcome by heedlessness that they are not ready to listen to any of his advice.

Fresh and continuous reminders:
It is stated that along with heedlessness and indifference, they also possess a sense of intoxication and mischief. Reminders are repeatedly presented to them through various aspects and styles, but whatever fresh reminder and warning comes to them, instead of listening to it seriously and pondering over it, they try to dismiss it in jest and mockery. In Surah Ta-Ha, verse 113, it is said: (And thus We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran and have diversified therein the warnings that perhaps they will avoid [evil] or it would cause them remembrance). The meaning is that Allah did not content Himself with letting them hear it just once, but sent fresh reminders to awaken them from the sleep of heedlessness. However, instead of being warned and appreciating them, they turn every reminder into an object of mockery.

The verbal fabrications of the Quraysh leaders:
"Lahiyatan qulubuhum" (their hearts distracted) is another attribute. The meaning is that the true function of the heart is reflection and remembrance, but their state is such that their hearts are so lost in their distractions that they try to laugh off even the most serious matters and the greatest truths.

Here, the words of the Quraysh leaders and their intellectuals are being quoted, which they used to spread among the people in their assemblies to stop the growing influence of the Quran and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). They realized well that the call of the Quran was making a home in people's hearts and that fair-minded people were being influenced by it. To erase this influence, they began to sow doubts among the people, saying, "Do not think that the power and influence in his speech is because he is an emissary. Do you not see that he is a human being just like you? If God wanted to send a messenger, He would have made an angel or some other creature His messenger, not a human being like us. And the power, impact, eloquence, and rhetoric you feel in his speech is merely the magic of this man's eloquence. Just as our other poets and orators influence people with their eloquence, this man is also trying to affect people with his verbal magic. So it would be great simplicity on your part if you knowingly fall into this man's magic!"

Since these rumors were devised by the leaders specifically in their private assemblies and spread from there, they are referred to as "Najwa" (secret counsels). The words "alladhina zalamu" (those who do wrong) indicate that those who sow these doubts are people who have wronged themselves by suspending their own intellects, and are wronging others by throwing dust in their eyes.

Meaning of "Sihr" (magic) and the reason for calling the Quran magic:
In "Afata'toona as-sihr" (Will you then approach magic?), magic refers to the discourse that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) recited to the people. The Arabs used to describe speech that possessed extraordinary influence and captivating power as magic. This expression is also present in our own language for speech that holds power and impact. As far as the eloquence, rhetoric, grandeur, and majesty of the Holy Quran and the Prophet's statements are concerned, they were such that even the opponents did not dare to deny them. Therefore, they were forced to admit it. However, they wanted to convince their common folk that this power and influence was not because it was a divine discourse or because its presenter was a messenger of God, but that it was entirely the sorcery of words and the magic of rhetoric. So, knowingly, seeing, and hearing, do not fall into this man's magic. The phrase "wa antum tubsiroon" (while you see) is meant to arouse a sense of superiority in the people, implying: "You are not gullible or simple people, but possessors of insight, so you should not fall for this deception."

Entrusting matters to Allah:
This is the description of the reaction of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to this nonsense of the opponents. He (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) knew that these rumors were devised to mislead the people by those to whom the truth of the Quran was fully clear. Therefore, without addressing them directly, he referred the matter to Allah, saying: "My Lord knows every word spoken in the heaven and the earth, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing." The meaning is that these whispers, in whichever corners of humans and jinn they take place, and these trials that people devise, my Lord knows everything well. So when He knows, He will also provide the means to address it. I entrust this matter to my Lord alone.

"Adghath" is the plural of "dighth". "Dighth" refers to a handful of grass that is a mixture of both wet and dry, fresh and withered. From this originates the idiom "adghathu ahlam", which means muddled dreams—that is, dreams that are completely devoid of meaning and, due to their confusion, are not worthy of any attention, interpretation, or significance.

It is stated that the matter did not stop at them calling the Prophet's revelation magic; rather, they went further to describe it as muddled dreams, deemed it a fabrication, called the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a poet, and demanded that if he is a prophet of God, he should show a sign as proof, just as the former prophets came with signs.

The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) did not present the Quran as his own speech but as the revelation of Allah, which was sent down to him through the medium of the archangel Gabriel. When the opponents felt that this claim regarding the Quran had greatly increased its significance and that people were accepting it not as human speech but as divine revelation, they began saying to counter this that the claim of this discourse being divine revelation is merely the creativity of imagination. It is a collection of muddled dreams. The thoughts that are deeply ingrained in this person's mind appear to him in his sleep as dreams, and he presents them (seeking refuge in Allah) to deceive the public with the claim that God has sent down revelation through an angel. It should be kept in mind that some modern atheists have also said the exact same thing to cast doubt on revelation as the ignorant of Arabia said. The only difference is that the ignorant of Arabia said it in an unrefined manner, while the claimants of intellect in this era have tried to present it in the garb of philosophy. If Allah wills, we will discuss this issue in detail in the last group of surahs.

Accusing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) of being a poet:
"Bal iftarahu"—meaning they also call this Quran a fabrication. By calling it a fabrication, their aim was to say that it is indeed the creation of his own mind, but (seeking refuge in Allah) he falsely attributes it to God to establish his authority over us.

"Bal huwa sha'ir"—meaning he is a poet, and all the spellbinding nature of this discourse of his is of the same kind as the spellbinding nature found in the poetry of our poets. The meaning is that there is no reason to give him and his discourse more importance than this. It should be kept in mind here that the conception of the Arabs was that every great poet has a jinn associated with him who inspires him with poetry. By calling the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a poet, they wanted to subject people to the delusion that, God forbid, he also had a jinn who inspired him with this discourse.

The opponents' awe of the Quran:
From these statements of the opponents of the Quran, one thing is absolutely clear: they were highly awed by its majesty and grandeur, and understood well that the awe of it could not be removed from people's hearts. However, their effort was to somehow reduce the awe of it being a divine discourse, so that people would not accord it the status of revelation and inspiration but keep it in the category of human and mortal speech.

Demand for the sign of miracles:
After "Kama ursila al-awwaloon" (as the former ones were sent), the word "bil-ayat" (with signs) is omitted based on contextual indication. That is, when they felt that their absurd talk regarding the Quran and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) would not settle in people's hearts, they demanded that if he is a messenger, he should also show a sign like those shown by the messengers who came before. By signs, they meant physical miracles or signs of punishment mentioned in the stories of other prophets. This weapon was, in their estimation, the most effective weapon in opposing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), because the call of the Quran was entirely based on the arguments of the horizons, the souls, intellect, and nature. Instead of extraordinary miracles and signs of punishment, it urged people to open their eyes and employ intellect and insight, showing that the natural path of faith is the path of the intellect and the heart. Those who do not use intellect and insight do not believe even after seeing extraordinary miracles, and the faith of those who believe only after seeing the sign of punishment is of no benefit. This statement of the Quran was absolutely true, but the opponents interpreted it as the Prophet's evasion to mislead the people, saying, "Look, if he were truly a messenger, what was so difficult for him to fulfill this demand of ours? But since he is evading it, it means that he is (God forbid) false in his claim."

Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
This is to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that he should not care about their nonsense. They have set out on the same path of obstinacy and stubbornness upon which the destroyed nations before them walked. Just as they denied their prophets and as a consequence We destroyed them, similarly, destruction has been decreed for these people too. They saw all kinds of signs but did not adopt the path of faith. So how do you expect from them that if a sign is shown to them according to their demand, they will become believers? Even after seeing everything, they will remain as blind as they currently are, blind and deaf!

Above in verse 3, the objection of the opponents was quoted: "Hal hadha illa basharun mithlukum" (Is this other than a human being like you?). This is the reply to that objection, stating that in the history of the world, this is not the first occurrence of God sending a human being as a messenger; rather, all the messengers who came before you were human beings, and God never sent a non-human as a messenger. The distinction that the messengers possessed was not that they were superhuman, but only that We sent Our revelation to them—the same kind of revelation We send to you, against which these people are uttering this nonsense.

Witness of the enemy:
In the above segment, the address was directed toward the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Here, addressing the opponents and deniers directly, it is said: "If you are unaware of this fact, then ask those who were given the Book before and are well-acquainted with the history of prophets and messengers." This is an allusion to the People of the Book, particularly the Jews. Making them witnesses is equivalent to making the enemy a witness, because in this period, as has become clear from previous surahs, the People of the Book as a community had entered the field to oppose the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and support the Quraysh. The Quran made them witnesses to establish conclusive proof over the Quraysh, implying: "Denial of this reality cannot be dared even by your supporters and the enemies of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the People of the Book, so why do you not ask them?" Here, referring to the People of the Book as "Ahl adh-Dhikr" (the people of the message) instead of "Ahl al-Kitab" has this eloquence: that being blinded in opposition to Islam and the Prophet of Islam is one thing, but those among them who remember their prophets and messengers cannot dare to deny this obvious truth.

A sarcasm on the Quraysh:
Within the words "in kuntum la ta'lamoon" (if you do not know), there is also a sarcasm directed at the Quraysh: that although you too should know this fact since you claim to be the descendants and heirs of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them), who were after all human beings and not superhuman, yet if you have forgotten this due to being unlettered, then refresh your memory by asking those who at least have not forgotten that all the messengers who came were human beings, and none was an angel.

No prophet was superhuman:
That is, all those prophets also came with the same human characteristics that you possess. Neither were they given bodies that were independent of the need for food and drink, nor did they come as immortal. They too ate and drank like humans, and they too had to face death just as every human must face it.

This objection is quoted elsewhere in this manner:
(Is this other than a human being like you? He eats of that which you eat and drinks of what you drink. And if you should obey a man like yourselves, indeed, you would then be losers. [Al-Mu'minun: 33-34])

"Wa ma kanoo khalideen" (nor were they immortal) is a refutation of their assumption that a messenger must be immortal. In this same Surah, this notion is refuted later in this manner:
(And We did not grant to any man before you eternity [on earth]; so if you die—would they be eternal? Every soul will taste death.)

Meaning, We have not granted eternal life to any human being before you, whether he be a prophet or a non-prophet. If you are to die, then they too are not to remain forever; every soul must taste death under all circumstances. The point is that death is an inevitable stage of this life from which no one is exempt; even a prophet must pass through this stage. This thing is neither detrimental to his prophethood, nor is it a matter of loss of dignity for others to accept him as a prophet.

By "Wa'd" (promise) is meant the same promise that Allah made to His messengers: that if people deny them, He will destroy the deniers and save them and their companions. We have explained under Jonah 20 that Allah's messengers warned their respective nations of two punishments: one is the punishment that inevitably comes in this world upon the nation that denies the messenger, and the other is the punishment that will be in the Hereafter. Here, punishment is meant. It is stated that they were indeed human beings, but history bears witness that We fulfilled the promise We made to them, meaning We saved them and those whom We willed, and destroyed those who exceeded the limits. Here, the style of expression "man nasha'u" (whom We willed) has made the speech correspond to the situation, meaning: "This is not just a tale of the past, but right now before you is this decisive stage. If you invite Our punishment through your obstinacy, We will save from it only those whom We will. No other person will be able to interfere in this Will of Ours, nor will anyone be able to escape Our grasp." By "Musrifeen" (the extravagant/transgressors) is meant those who defiantly break Allah's limits and mock the warnings of the messenger. These people are established as rebels against God, and the suppression of rebels is inevitable.

Meaning, just as We sent messengers to the past nations for their reminder before destroying them so that proof might be established against them, similarly, We have sent down to you a Book in which We have fully reminded you. Now you cannot make the excuse before Us that you were not reminded. This reminder carries its inevitable results which will surely manifest in accordance with the Divine Sunnah. This is not the request of a petitioner which, if refused, will be set aside; rather, rejecting it will bring the same result before you that came before those who preceded you. The Sunnah of God that has been in force until today will not change in your case. "Afala ta'qiloon" (Will you not then understand?) is used here in the context of severe threat and warning: "O foolish ones, where has your intellect gone! Why are you inviting your own doom!" To understand the true force of this verse, take a look at the explanation provided under verses 133-135 of Surah Ta-Ha.

Reference to history:
This points to the history of past nations, meaning: "Just as you are bringing ruin upon yourselves by mocking Allah's reminder, similarly, many nations before you committed the same act, as a consequence of which We shattered them into pieces." The meaning is that if you are guilty of the same crime, there is no reason why God should not deal with you as He dealt with them.

"Kanat zalimah" (it was wrongdoing) here carries the meaning of "zalaloo anfusahum" (they wronged themselves); that is, Allah did not do any wrong to them, but they themselves became wrongdoers to their own souls. God warned them of the danger by sending His messengers, but out of their arrogance, they themselves broke the barrier of that danger.

Refutation of a delusion of nations:
"Wa ansha'na ba'daha qawman akhareen"—meaning for God, to erase one nation and establish another nation in its place is not a difficult task. Let no one consider their existence indispensable for this world, thinking that if they are ruined, God's world will go to ruin. When any nation adopts the path of rebellion, God will erase it and bring another nation in its place, and see what path they adopt. If they too adopt the same path, their end will ultimately be the same... Whether individuals or nations, when indifference toward God overcomes them, they begin to give excessive importance to their own existence. Here, this delusion is dispelled: "Do not think of yourselves as something very great; whenever God wills, He will sweep this place clean and settle others in your stead."

No refuge from the grasp of God:
That is, just as today you are inviting God's punishment with great pomp and arrogance, similarly, they too invited God's punishment with arrogance. Ultimately, when Our punishment knocked at their doors, they could devise no plan against it; instead, they fled from there. But no one can flee from God's punishment. When He grasps, no one can escape His clutches!

A sarcastic style:
This is the description of the situation; meaning, the grasp of God said to them through the language of its state: "Where do you flee now? Do not flee now; the time for fleeing has passed! Go back to the luxuries in which you used to make merry till now and to your palaces and mansions where you sat and mocked the verses of God and His messenger, so that you may be thoroughly questioned!" Here, "La'allakum tus'aloon" (that you may be questioned) is in the sense of sarcasm and mockery. Sardonically it is said: "Wait, now I shall question you, now I shall take account of you." This style is used in other places in the Quran as well, such as in verse 8 of At-Takathur and verse 92 of An-Nahl.

From the style of expression "ila ma utriftum feehi" (to that in which you were given luxury), the point emerges that this luxury and abundance of blessings was granted to you by God so that your gratitude might be tested, but you considered it the inheritance of your ancestors, your personal entitlement, and the result of your own capability, and on its strength, you rebelled against the very God by whose grace you obtained these blessings. So now taste the consequence of it. Since the address here is to the wealthy and affluent, and the dwellings are mentioned after the mention of their means of luxury and comfort, "masakin" (dwellings) refers to the mansions and palaces of these very wealthy people.

Verses 14-15:
Belated confession:
That is, when the punishment enveloped them, only then did they regain consciousness and begin to wail, crying out: "Indeed, we were ourselves wrongdoers, and we brought this doom upon ourselves." We have already pointed out this meaning of the word "Zulm" under verse 11. They had to make this confession because before this punishment, God's messenger had warned them well, but they not only remained blind and deaf but also demanded it with great defiance. Ultimately, when it arrived upon them, they had to admit that the entire fault was their own.

"Fama zalat tilka da'wahum" (And that did not cease to be their cry) until the end of the verse. "Da'wa" means crying out, seeking help, and lamentation. Here it refers to their same wailing and crying which was mentioned in the previous verse. That is, they kept crying and wailing in this manner, but they were not listened to, because after the arrival of punishment, crying and wailing are of no avail. Consequently, We made them like harvested grass and extinguished fire. In the metaphor of harvested grass and extinguished fire, the theme is implied that just as grass is cut down, fire is set to its dry heap, and it is reduced to a heap of ashes, similarly We turned them into dust and ashes. The word "khamideen" (extinguished) is used here in keeping with the metaphorical association, and this is a well-known style in Arabic.

The upcoming theme... Verses 16-29:
Moving forward, the proof of the matter mentioned above is stated: that the reckoning of nations in this world, and what will occur in the Hereafter, is because God has not made this world as a plaything or a theater for His amusement, as worldly kings build theaters for their amusement; rather, He has created it for the manifestation of His grace, mercy, truth, and justice. The inevitable demand of this grace, mercy, truth, and justice is that His servants should not lead an unbridled life but live bound to God's system of truth and justice, and that this world should not just go on like this indefinitely, but be followed by a Day of Reckoning wherein people receive the reward of their performance—those who lived according to the will of the Creator of this world are rewarded, and those who created disorder therein meet their end. If this world were to continue like this without such a Day of Reckoning, it would mean that its Creator is some playful entity who has staged this play merely for His amusement, having no concern for its good, evil, justice, or injustice. This notion is obviously false and completely contrary to God's attributes of justice and mercy; therefore, the Resurrection is inevitable. In this connection, association of partners (shirk) is also refuted, because if any room for shirk is left, then in that case too, this world becomes a child's play and a game. The reason for this is that if the existence of such partners and intercessors is accepted who interfere so much in God's affairs that they can save their worshippers from God's grasp, whatever their deeds may be, then the occurrence or non-occurrence of the Resurrection becomes equal. It would mean that even if this day of justice came, there is no need for any worry or preparation; escape from this court can be obtained through intercessors, and these assumed partners and intercessors are sufficient for them for intercession. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Proof of the necessity of reward and punishment:
What has been stated above regarding the necessity of reward and punishment, its proof is stated here: that if there is no Day of Reward and Punishment behind this world, and it has just been going on like this and will always go on like this—whoever does good or evil, injustice or justice, and its Creator has no concern with its good and evil—then it means that He has made this merely as a plaything for His amusement. As long as He wills, He will amuse Himself with it, and when He gets tired, He will fold its carpet and create some new game for His interest and pastime. It is stated: "We have not created this heaven and earth and what is between them as a plaything." This workshop of the universe bears witness through its existence that it is a workshop made by an All-Wise, All-Just, and Most Merciful Creator. It is neither the play of a playful entity, nor the battlefield of demigods, nor the 'lila' of some Bhagwan; rather, every single thing of this wise workshop bears witness that its Creator has created it with a great purpose and objective, and the obvious demand of His justice and wisdom is that a day should come in which His purpose is manifested.

Play and vain work are contrary to God's attributes, but even if hypothetically We were to do so, We would have provided its means from Ourselves. Why would We drag Our servants, maidservants, and other creatures into it! We have mentioned in this book regarding the ancient Roman emperors that they used to watch the spectacle of hungry lions and their oppressed slaves tearing each other apart in their theaters. God forbid, the same opinion would have to be formed regarding the Creator of this universe if He were putting all His creatures into this trial just to watch their spectacle. It is obvious that in this world there are both oppressors and the oppressed, the rebellious and the loyal, the ungrateful and the grateful; in such a state, if there is no Day of Justice for this world, then it is like a theater of the Roman emperors. In this verse, this absurd notion is refuted, showing that the relationship God has with His creatures makes it necessary that He should bring a day in which the decision between truth and falsehood is made, truth is exalted, and falsehood is destroyed. If this does not happen, this world remains a play, and this is contrary to God's attributes.

"Law ittakhadhna lahwalan lattakhadhnahu min ladunna" (Had We intended to take a plaything, We could have taken it from Us)—within these few words, the reality pointed out is that if We wanted to make a plaything, We would have provided its means from Our own presence. There was no need to establish this battlefield of Adam and Iblis, truth and falsehood, guidance and error, justice and injustice, good and evil, and oppressor and oppressed. It is completely contrary to God's justice and mercy that He should send His servants and maidservants into a battlefield and Himself become a mere spectator, neither rewarding those who succeed in this struggle nor punishing those who spent their entire lives in the worship of falsehood and its support.

It is stated: First, this kind of play and vain work is contrary to Our majesty and Our high attributes, but even if hypothetically We were to do so...

"In kunna fa'ileen" (if We were to do so) refutes this false conception regarding God. It should be kept in mind that based on this false conception, the polytheistic nations of the world turned this universe into a theater of their demigods, and Hindu philosophers described it as the 'lila' of Bhagwan. Since they did not have a clear conception of the Resurrection, they thought that just as worldly kings make theaters for their amusement, similarly their unseen demigods made this world and they watch its spectacle by making someone an oppressor, someone oppressed, someone a killer, and someone killed to amuse themselves. Although the deniers and atheists of our time do not say this in words because it sounds very crude, yet when they do not believe in the Resurrection and a Day of Justice and Equity, the same false conception is ingrained in their hearts too, because the denial of Resurrection, in other words, means that this world is a child's play. Without believing in the Resurrection, no such explanation of this universe is possible which can establish it as based on truth and justice. There is the same relationship between the two as exists between spouses. If we assume one member of the pair apart from its other part and try to explain it and find the wisdom of its occurrences and demands, it is impossible.

Statement of the actual reality:
"Damgh" means to strike and wound someone in such a way that it brain-shatters him.

It is stated: "You who think of this world as a plaything, this notion is completely false. The respite We have given to falsehood alongside truth in it is not because truth and falsehood are equal in Our sight, but it is merely for your trial to see who adopts the path of truth and who becomes a worshipper of falsehood. Ultimately, a day will come when you will see that We will strike the hammer of truth on the head of falsehood in such a way that it will shatter its brain, and just as you see that a fatal blow to the brain finishes a man instantly, similarly this fatal blow will destroy falsehood in the blink of an eye."

"Wa lakumu al-waylu mimma tasifoon" (And woe to you for what you describe)—meaning at that time, because of this nonsense you are uttering today, there will be nothing but destruction and ruin for you. By "mimma tasifoon" (what you describe), in our view, is meant both their conception of the world being a mere plaything and their belief in shirk and intercession due to which they were completely carefree of the Hereafter, thinking that even if they had to face the Hereafter, their partners and intercessors would save them from every danger. It is stated: "All these notions of yours will become nothing but a cause of plague and destruction for you."

Verses 19-20:
Refutation of the delusion of Shirk:
In the previous verses, the necessity of reward and punishment was argued from the divine attributes. Now comes the statement of Monotheism (Tawheed) so that the reality may be clarified: that if anyone has imagined that they will escape God's grasp and reward and punishment through the intercession of their partners and intercessors, this is merely their delusion which has no reality. It is stated: "And to Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth." All are the creation of God and His possession; no one possesses any authority or power in God's presence.

The state of the angels:
"Wa man 'indahu la yastakbiroona 'an 'ibadatihi wa la yastahsiroon" (And those who are near Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they tire). By "man 'indahu" (those near Him) is meant the close angels of God. It is stated: "They too are included in this category. Despite all their closeness and honor, their state is such that they never turn away from His worship, nor do they ever tire of it." This is a refutation of the polytheists' delusion who assumed the angels to be the daughters of God and worshipped them. Their assumption was that they possessed a great status and position in God's presence, they could get whatever they wanted accepted by their father, and God surely complied with their every wish in pampering them. They considered their worldly blessings to be their gift, and regarding the Hereafter, they thought that even if rising after death occurred, they would obtain a high status there because of them. It is stated: "You carry yourself arrogantly against God on their strength, but their own state is such that they never turn away from God's servitude, nor do they feel any fatigue in this work; they glorify God night and day, not pausing for a single moment."

Verses 21-22:
"Yunsheroon al-ard" means God revived the earth and made it fresh and green after it had become dry and barren. For example, He said: (And He who sends down rain from the sky in measured amounts, and We give life thereby to a dead land...) [Az-Zukhruf: 11]

The Lord of the earth and heaven is One:
This is a refutation of another polytheistic delusion of the polytheists. Many polytheists believed that the gods of the earth and the heaven are separate. They believed in one Lord of the Throne, but their idea was that the Lord of the Throne is seated on His heavenly throne, while in the earth, the godship of other gods is running. Under this delusion, they became indifferent to the God of heaven and worshipped their earthly deities. From the study of the mythology of the pagan Arabs, it is known that their conception was that since the earth is a very distant region of the kingdom of the Lord of the Throne, He has handed over its management and administration to others and detached Himself from it. It is stated: "Have they taken gods from the earth who raise the dead and make it green and fresh? Their idea is completely false. If there were in the heavens and the earth gods other than Allah, both would have been ruined." After all, what would have prompted those in heaven to send down water to make the earth green and fresh after its dryness and barrenness, or to participate in providing their other needs? We have explained this argument of Tawheed under the title of "Argument of Harmony" in another place. Repeating it here would cause length. Its summary is that the survival of this universe depends on the fact that there is a very deep harmony among its different parts, elements, and all its opposites. If this harmony clearly testifies that a single entity's Will (Mind) is active over this entire universe. If many wills were active in it, the survival of heaven and earth would have been impossible.

"Fasubhana Allahi rabbi al-'arshi 'amma yasifoon" (So exalted is Allah, Lord of the Throne, above what they describe)—after stating this argument of Tawheed, its result is placed forward: that this kind of conception regarding God, the Lord of the Throne, is a foolish conception. He is far above and pure from such things that they describe. Since He is the Owner of the throne of governance of this universe, only His command runs in heaven and earth.

All are accountable before God:
Here Allah has clarified His majesty: that He is not questioned or accountable before anyone, while all others, whether angels, jinn, or humans, are accountable before Him. No one has the audacity to turn away from His command, and if anyone dares to do so, he will have to answer in the presence of Allah and will suffer the punishment for this audacity. This is a refutation of the polytheists' delusion who, considering their deities to be partners in the administration of this universe, expected from them that they could change whichever of God's wills they wanted and do whatever they wished of their own accord, being above God's questioning.

Verses 24-25:
The testimony of history in establishing Monotheism and refuting Shirk:
This is a reference to the testimony of history in establishing Monotheism and refuting Shirk. This is a separate and independent argument from the above-mentioned "Argument of Harmony"; therefore, the question "Am ittakhadhoo min doonihi alihatan" (Or have they taken besides Him gods?) has been repeated. It is stated: "If they believe in other deities besides God, then tell them to bring some proof for this." This demand for proof from the supporters of Shirk is on the basis that as far as God is concerned, He is not a matter of dispute and disagreement; even they believe in Him, so He is an accepted reality. As for the claim that there are other deities besides Him, this requires proof, and the responsibility to prove it lies upon the claimants of this claim. If they have any proof for this, they should present it.

"Hadha dhikru man ma'iya wa dhikru man qabli"—meaning this Quran contains that teaching which has been given to me and those with me (i.e., my Ummah), and it contains that teaching which was sent down to the prophets before me. All have taught this very Tawheed; none has taught Shirk. If you have any testimony contrary to this, then bring it.

"Bal aktharuhum la ya'lamoona al-haqqa fahum mu'ridoon"—meaning it is not that they have some proof on the basis of which they have rolled up their sleeves in opposition to Tawheed and support of Shirk; rather, it is only ignorance and unawareness of the truth that is the cause of all this turning away and denial. This is to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions that they should not be grieved by this storm of opposition. When the basis of opposition is not argument and proof but entirely unawareness and ignorance, then what is the cure for this disease!

"Wa ma arsalna min qablika min rasoolin illa noohee ilayhi annahu la ilaha illa ana fa'budoon"—this is the explanation of the above phrase "dhikru man qabli" (the message of those before me). We have not sent any messenger before you except that We revealed to him: "There is no deity except Me, so worship Me." It should be clear that although countless alterations have occurred in the scrolls of the past prophets, the teaching of Tawheed is still preserved in them. If their followers adopted Shirk, they did so on the strength of their corrupted theology, not on the basis of the teaching of those scrolls, just as despite the very clear teaching of Tawheed in the Quran, many types of Shirk have entered this Ummah. Similarly, those nations adopted the curse of Shirk in complete opposition to the teaching of their scrolls and then tried to provide external arguments for it. We have given references to the teachings of the Torah, Gospel, Psalms, etc., in previous surahs. In the Torah, the teaching of Abraham is also present, which is entirely Tawheed. In short, the recorded teachings and calls of the prophets (peace be upon them) confirm this claim of the Quran that every messenger of Allah taught Tawheed, and none taught Shirk. Whoever claims contrary to this is completely ignorant of the history of the prophets and their call.

Verses 26-27:
The status of the angels in God's court:
This is a refutation of the polytheists' false belief of intercession, who made the angels of God His daughters and were under the delusion that whatever sustenance and grace they receive in this world is due to their favor, and if there is a Hereafter, they will obtain very high statuses there through their intercession. The refutation of this false belief here, as we have pointed out above, is from the aspect that if this kind of intercession were accepted in Allah's presence, the occurrence or non-occurrence of the Hereafter becomes equal, because when in God's court, truth can be made falsehood and falsehood made truth through bribery or intercession, then this world remains a place of darkness. Where then is this workshop based on truth and justice! And who will accept its Creator as Just and Wise!

It is stated: "This idea of theirs is completely false. Allah, glorified be His name, is pure and exalted above daughters, sons, and wives. Such relationships are completely contrary to His status of Divinity. The angels are not the daughters of God, as these foolish people think, but they are His honored servants. They possess the status of closeness to God, but this closeness is obtained because they are completely pure from evil and perfect in God's servitude and loyalty, not because they can get whatever they want accepted by God."

"La yasbiqoonahu bil-qawli wa hum bi-amrihi ya'maloon"—meaning let alone getting something accepted through force, influence, or coyness, they do not even initiate speech before God. When they are permitted, only then do they open their mouths, and they answer whatever is asked within the limits of respect. Nor do they possess the capacity to initiate any action on their own; they merely execute the command given to them by God. So, for those whose status before God is this, to pin hopes on them that they will release whomsoever they want from God's grasp by their force and influence, or elevate them to whatever rank and status they wish, is merely a foolish wish.

"Ya'lamu ma bayna aydeehim wa ma khalfahum"—meaning what is before them, behind them, present, and in their future, God Himself knows all these things well; therefore, neither can they add anything to God's knowledge regarding anyone, nor can they decrease or increase anything.

"Wa la yashfa'oona illa limani artada"—then this is also the case: they will not initiate intercession on their own for anyone, but will intercede only for those whom God approves.

"Wa hum min khashyatihi mushfiqoon"—furthermore, they are always fearful and trembling out of fear of God; therefore, there is no possibility that they will utter any word contrary to the truth.

The point is that those who are relying on their intercession should think over all these things, and not ruin their Hereafter on the strength of a mere delusion.

Meaning: "You have given them such a status as if God is a toy in their hands, they can play with Him as they wish, but the actual reality—listen to it with open ears—is that if even one of them were to say that he too is a deity besides God, We would throw him into Hell. This is the punishment of Hell that We have prescribed for all polytheists, and We will surely give it to them too." It will be remembered that the exact same thing was said by Allah addressing the Christians regarding Jesus and his respected mother.

The upcoming theme... Verses 30-33:
In the previous verses, those arguments of the Return (Ma'ad) and Monotheism (Tawheed) were stated which are based on the obvious facts and demands of intellect, nature, and divine attributes. Now, those demanding signs, mentioned in verse 5, are directed toward the signs of the horizons, asking: "Why do they demand some new sign? This universe of God is full of the signs of His power, mercy, providence, monotheism, and return. Why do they not ponder over these signs? When they have closed their eyes to all these signs, what is the guarantee that if a new sign is shown, their eyes will open? The true path for guidance to enter the heart is the intellect; when they have bandaged their intellect, how can they obtain guidance?" In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

The testimony of the horizons on Monotheism, the Return, and Reward:
"Kana" is used here in the same sense as it is in "Kana Allahu 'aleeman hakeema". "Ratq" means closed, and "Fatq" means opening. By the closing and opening of heaven and earth, the aim is to draw attention to the fact: "Do you see that the sky gets closed, no rain falls from it, similarly the earth gets closed, no greenery grows from it? Then you see that the sky opens, and water begins to pour from it, and after that, God opens the earth too, and it begins to bring out its treasures of vegetation. Until yesterday, the earth was completely dry and dead, but with the rain, signs of life appeared in every corner of it."

It is stated: "Those who are denying Monotheism and the Return and demanding some sign to believe—why do they not ponder over these signs of the horizons which are coming under their observation every day? Allah displayed these signs in His universe so that people might obtain guidance to the right path from them!"

If you ponder, you will realize that within this single observation, the arguments for all those things to which the Quran is calling them are present.

In it, a very clear argument of Monotheism is present. If there were a separate god in heaven and a separate deity in earth, what would have prompted the heaven to spend its water reservoir to keep the earth green and fresh? This marital compatibility between heaven and earth is a clear testimony that the Creator and Owner of both is One, and His Will is active on both.

The second testimony in it is of the Return. When the dry and barren earth, or in other words, dead earth, is revived and made fresh again, why should rising again after death be considered far-fetched!

The third testimony in it is of the human being being accountable. When God has made such arrangements for the nourishment of man that He keeps His heaven, earth, sun, moon, and clouds active for his sake, how is it possible that He should leave him completely unbridled, without prescribing any day of reckoning for him?

This same theme is stated in Surah Qaf in this manner:
(And We have sent down blessed rain from the sky and made grow thereby gardens and grain from the harvest, and lofty palm trees having fruit arranged in layers—as provision for the servants, and We have given life thereby to a dead land. Thus is the resurrection.) [Qaf: 9-11]

Great signs like mountains:
"Rawasi" in Arabic comes as an attribute for mountains, and this attribute has become so famous that it is used in place of the noun. In "an tameeda bihim", before "an", a word like "karahiyata" or its synonym is omitted. "Fijaj" is the plural of "fajj". "Fajj" means the cleft between two mountains.

It is stated: "We have set mountains on the earth which keep its balance stable, lest it tilt to any direction with them and collide with some other sphere. We also made this arrangement that We created passes between these mountains to serve as paths for people, so that they may travel from one region to another and from one country to another. If God had not done so, people would have remained locked up within their own regions, and it would have been in no one's power to break these walls and open paths of travel and trade."

Within "La'allahum yahtadoon" (that perhaps they will be guided), both the meaning that people may obtain guidance from these paths and the meaning that God displayed these great signs so that seeing them, people's eyes might open, and the power, majesty, and wisdom of the Creator of the mountains might become clear to them, and they might find the path to God, are present. But this is a strange blindness of man: he does not obtain any guidance from seeing a great sign like a mountain, but demands that if some miracle is shown to him according to his request, he will adopt the path of guidance. After all, one who is unable to see the mountain, how will he see a sesame seed! In Surah Qaf it is said:
(And the earth—We have spread it out and cast therein firmly set mountains and made grow therein [something] of every beautiful kind, giving insight and a reminder for every servant who turns [to Allah].) [Qaf: 7-8]

From the last words of this verse, it is clear that the actual purpose of showing these great signs is that insight should be created in people, their eyes should open, and they should obtain remembrance. But these signs are beneficial for those who turn toward them. Those who do not possess the capacity to turn remain as blind as before even after seeing everything.

Pointer to the sky and its signs:
After pointing to some of the prominent signs of the earth, attention is directed toward the sky. It is stated: "After all, why do those demanding signs not look at this boundless blue roof of the sky, the limit of whose width and breadth is not known, yet despite this, not the slightest defect can be pointed out in it? No one knows when this roof was made, but despite this lapse of time, neither has any oldness overcome it, nor has any defect or flaw appeared in it. Completely safe from every kind of crack and split, it stands firm."

This same theme is stated in Surah Qaf thus:
(Have they not looked at the heaven above them—how We structured it and adorned it and [how] it has no rifts?) [Qaf: 6]

In Surah Al-Mulk, this same matter is stated thus:
([And] who created seven heavens in layers. You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any inconsistency. So return [your] vision; do you see any breaks? Then return [your] vision twice again. [Your] vision will return to you humbled while it is fatigued [and find no defect].) [Al-Mulk: 3-4]

If further explanation of this theme is required, take a look at Al-Isra 99 and An-Nazi'at 27-33. Some have taken "mahfooz" (protected) to mean that the sky is protected from the devil's intrusion. This statement is correct in itself, but it has a specific context. Here, this matter is not under discussion. On this issue, if God wills, we will discuss in the commentary of Surah Al-Mulk.

In "Wa hum 'an ayatiha mu'ridoon" (And they turn away from its signs), pointer is made to the reality that as far as signs are concerned, there is no lack of them—every single thing in this universe is a sign—but they have closed their eyes to the signs, so what is the cure for such blind ones!

After pointing to the sky, attention is directed toward the prominent signs of the sky: that He is the One who created the night, day, sun, and moon. Although there is an opposition between them in terms of their nature, they are active in the service of this universe with complete harmony and perfect agreement. This is a testimony that their rein is in the hand of a single absolute power which has subjected them for the collective benefit of the world.

"Kullun fee falakin yasbahoon"—by "kullun" (all) is meant the sun and the moon. We have mentioned in another place that the word "kullun" when it comes in this manner is in the ruling of a definite noun, and it refers to the previously mentioned things, whether they are two or more. It is stated: "This sun and moon move in their respective determined orbits, they cannot deviate from them by a hair's breadth; if they were to move even slightly here or there, defect would occur in the entire system of the universe." Their remaining engaged in their duty with the compliance of minutes and seconds, and always remaining engaged without any slight failure or pause, is a clear testimony that they are subjects of the Sovereign of the universe and subjected in His hand. Now, what a loss of intellect it is that despite these signs, which they observe in the light of day and the darkness of night, they demand some other sign! And even greater is the foolishness of those who have made the sun and moon deities, while they are night and day proclaiming their being subjects and subjected!

The upcoming theme... Verses 34-47:
Take a look back at verse 8. The matter started from here: that the opponents were mocking the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) on the basis: "How can you be a messenger when you too are a human being just as common humans are, and you do not possess any distinction in terms of wealth and status?" Then they said: "If we are to accept your messengership, you must show some sign which proves that indeed your statement is true." In the previous verses, the demand for signs was answered. Now in the upcoming verses, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is being consoled: "Do not care about the mockery of these people. The time is coming when they will see the truth of your every word with their own eyes, and all their pride will be finished." In this light, recite the verses.

Verses 34-35:
Answer to those objecting to the human nature of the messenger:
In verse 3, the objection of the disbelievers against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) has passed: "Hal hadha illa basharun mithlukum" (Is this other than a human being like you?)—meaning, just as you eat and drink, he too eats and drinks, and just as you will die one day, he too will die one day, so what is the meaning of accepting a human being like yourselves as a prophet and messenger? There, this objection was answered from another aspect. Here, this objection is approached again, striking another blow at the inner thoughts of the objectors. It is stated: "To be a prophet and messenger, it is not necessary to be immortal. If death comes to you, then before you, it came to every human being, whether he was a prophet or a non-prophet, and it will come to everyone. None has passed before you who was protected from this thing. If you will die one day, they too are not to remain forever; they too must inevitably face this death one day. This death is an accompaniment of human nature; its taste must be tasted by everyone." The point is: in their objection, some weight could have been there only if the Prophet had come with eternal life. At that time, they could have said: "How can we accept a mortal human being as a messenger when we are the masters of eternal life?" But when he too is mortal, why do they demand an immortal messenger!

For a messenger, neither is being superhuman necessary, nor being wealthy:
"Wa nablookum bish-sharri wal-khayri fitnatan wa ilayna turja'oon"—this is the answer to another aspect of the objection. Just as the disbelievers objected to the Prophet's human nature, they also said: "If God wanted to make a human being a messenger, He would have made some chief of Makkah or Ta'if a messenger. Why did He make him a messenger who is a poor man devoid of wealth and status?" The Quran has answered this gibe in different places from various aspects. Here too, the answer is given in a new style. It is stated: "Just as for a messenger, being superhuman is not necessary, similarly his being a possessor of wealth and property is also not necessary. In this world, wealth is neither a proof of someone being close to God, nor is poverty a proof of him being contemptible. In this world, poverty and wealth, pain and pleasure, grief and comfort are only for trial. Through them, Allah tests His servants and distinguishes their pure from the impure. When He gives wealth and status to someone, He tests his gratitude, and when He gives poverty, He tests his patience. Then the return of all will be to God alone, and He will decide who earned and who lost. Those who, on obtaining wealth, consider themselves the favorites of God to the extent that they begin to think of prophethood and messengership as their monopoly, are completely mistaken."

In the verse, the word "fitnatan" is taken by Zamakhshari in the sense of emphasis of the verb, but in my view, it is a direct object denoting purpose (maf'ool lahu). I have kept this in view in the translation. The research on this word has passed in the commentary of Surah Ta-Ha.

Jealous for clay idols, but shameless for the Merciful God:
This refers to the manner and tone in which they mocked the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). It is stated: "These deniers of Monotheism and Resurrection mock you wherever they see you, saying in a highly contemptuous tone: 'Is this the one who mentions your gods?'" Mentions them, meaning mentions them with evil. Out of jealousy and zeal, they omitted the word "bis-soo'" (with evil), as if they could not tolerate bringing the word of evil for their idols on their tongues even to quote disbelief. In "yadhkuru alihatakum", the pronoun of address is to instigate their common folk; they did not say "he satirizes our gods" but "he satirizes your gods." The difference between the two styles is obvious.

"Wa hum bidhikri ar-rahmani hum kafiroon"—it is stated: "For the idols made of clay and stone, they possess this zeal and support that wherever they see Our messenger, they go after him like thorns of a bush; but in the matter of the True Deity, the Merciful God, their sense has died in such a way that let alone zeal and support, they are not even tolerant of His mention." It should be kept in mind here that those who are involved in some Shirk, their hearts become so upside down that for their partners, their sense is so sharp that in their zeal and support, they remain ready to fight with anyone, but in the matter of God, they are completely shameless. If someone, God forbid, insults God in their presence, no movement occurs in them! There is a specific psychological reason for this, to which the Quran has pointed in some places. At some appropriate place, its explanation will come.

Verses 37-38:
Answer to the haste for punishment:
By "insan" (man) is meant these very mocking people. These uncivilized people are mentioned with a general term. It is stated: "To humiliate the Prophet and his companions, these people also say: 'Where is this punishment you are threatening us with? If you are truthful, show it to us so that this dispute may be decided.'" In reply, it is stated: "This delay is happening by the grace of God; He wants those who, instead of benefiting from this opportunity, are making haste for punishment to reflect." After this, addressing them in a threatening tone, it is said: "If you are seekers of punishment, do not make haste; I will very soon show you My signs of punishment, from which it will become clear to you that what the Prophet is warning you of will surely come before you in this world too, and in the Hereafter too you will face it."

Verse 39:
This is said in a tone of sorrow: "Today they are demanding the punishment with such defiance and making haste for it. Would that they could estimate that time when the fire of God's punishment will envelop them from before and behind, and their helplessness will be such that neither will they be able to ward it off from their faces, nor from their backs, nor will any help come to them from those supporters on whose assembly they pride themselves, nor will they obtain any support from those deities on whose strength they are arrogant against God!"

It is stated: "When it comes, it will not come with a announced time. Its exact time is neither known to anyone nor can be known. For this reason, do not ask when it will come! When its time arrives, it will come upon them suddenly. At that time, neither will it be possible for them to ward it off, nor will they be given any respite after that to take a breath or make repentance and reform to escape from it."

Reference to history to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
This is a reference to history to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "The messengers of Allah who passed before you—their warnings were also mocked in the same manner as your warnings are being mocked. Ultimately, the result was that the very punishment they mocked enveloped those who mocked it." The meaning is that the same bad day is going to come before your mocking opponents too.

From "sakhiroo minhum" (mocked them), this fact becomes clear: that in the consequence of denying the messengers, in the decisive punishment that comes upon those nations, only those are destroyed who persist in their denial. We have pointed to this Divine Sunnah in other places.

A question to those making haste for punishment:
It is stated: "Ask those who are making such haste for punishment: 'What arrangements have they made to escape from it? After all, who is protecting them night and day to save them from the grasp of the Merciful God?' If God's punishment comes by night or by day, no one can stop it; so with what pomp they are demanding it, what army do they possess against it!

"Bal hum 'an dhikri rabbihim mu'ridoon"—this is an expression of sorrow over their wretchedness, showing that the building of all their pomp is established on sand. It is not that they have made some arrangement to escape from God's grasp on the strength of which they are arrogant, but being indifferent to the end, they have turned away from God's reminder.

Meaning: "Do they have gods besides Us to patronize and protect them, who will save them from Our grasp? If they are involved in this raw greed, they should know that let alone helping them, those deities will not be able to help even themselves against God, nor will any other help and companionship be obtained by them." It should be kept in mind here that the self-carved idols of the polytheists had no reality at all; if hypothetically any had an existence, it is said regarding them: "They will not be able to save themselves from God, so how will they save others!"

The actual cause of arrogance and turning away:
This unveils the actual cause of their arrogance and turning away: that enjoying God's blessings for a long period has resulted in their hearts becoming hard and dark. They have begun to think that the honor and power they possess is the inheritance of their ancestors, they are its hereditary rightful owners, in it they were born, in it they will make merry, and its heirs will be their descendants. This false assumption has made them completely indifferent to the Prophet's warnings. They think that all the Prophet's warnings are merely imaginary, and no one can challenge their power.

Enjoying God's blessings for a long period has manifested on the ungrateful in the form of hardness of heart. It is mentioned in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 16:
(...and they not be like those who were given the Scripture before, and a long period passed over them, so their hearts hardened.)

"Afala yarawna anna na'tee al-arda nanqusuha min atrafiha"—by "al-ard" (the land) here is meant the land of Makkah. In Surah Al-Hajj, where Muslims are permitted to wage jihad to free the land of Sanctuary from the control of the disbelievers, it is said: (Verse 41) (We will surely help those who, if We give them authority in the land, establish prayer and give zakah and enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, and to Allah belongs the outcome of [all] matters.) The context is proof that here "al-ard" has come for the land of Sanctuary; the exact same theme is stated in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 41. There we have shed light on its different aspects in detail.

In the verse under discussion, pointer is made to those signs which had manifested in that period for the dominance of Islam. In the struggle between truth and falsehood going on in Makkah, within Makkah itself, truth was still oppressed, but in the surrounding tribes, and particularly in Madinah, the call of Islam was taking root, from which it was gradually becoming clear that the sphere of influence of disbelief was reducing, and Islam was slowly surrounding Makkah from its borders to bring Makkah under its control. Pointing to this gradual dominance of Islam, it is said: "Do these people not see that We are gradually reducing their control from the borders of Makkah and advancing toward Makkah?"

"Afahumu al-ghaliboon"—meaning, do these clues testify that in this struggle they will remain dominant, or do they testify that Islam and its standard-bearers will remain dominant? If a sign is indeed demanded, why do these people not look at those signs which are manifesting in their own surroundings!

Verses 45-46:
It is stated: "Tell those who are demanding some sign of punishment from you: 'Instead of showing you any such sign, I am only warning you through revelation, so that instead of falling into some calamity, you may use your intellect and insight and think of escaping from God's wrath.' This thing was beneficial and worthy of gratitude for you, but instead of appreciating it, you are inviting your own doom!"

"Wa la yasma'u as-summu ad-du'a'a idha ma yundharoon"—this is to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "If these people are not appreciating this blessing, it is their own deprivation. They are deaf of intellect and heart, and those who are deaf do not hear the call of the caller when they are warned of some danger, until they fall into the ditch from which they are warned."

"Wa la'in massat-hum nafhatun..."—meaning today they are demanding the punishment with such defiance as if they stand fully armed to battle with it, but all this pride of theirs is only until it does not manifest. If even a slight whiff of divine wrath came from any direction, all this pride would disappear; then they would beat their heads, crying: "Woe to our wretchedness! We were ourselves wrongdoers to our own souls that instead of benefiting from the Prophet's revelation, we demanded punishment from him." The indefinite form of "nafhatun" (a whiff) here indicates that God's punishment is a big thing—if even a common whiff of it touched them, it would take out all their strength!

The Resurrection is for the manifestation of justice:
In "al-mawazeena al-qista liyawmi al-qiyamah", commentators have taken the "L" in the sense of "Fi" (in), but in my view, it is in the sense of objective and purpose. That is, for the purpose of the manifestation of the Resurrection, We will set up the scales of justice. It should be kept in mind that the Resurrection is not an end in itself; rather, it is necessary for the manifestation of God's justice and equity.

In "wa in kana", the noun of "kana" is omitted based on contextual clarity, and the feminine pronoun in "atayna biha" corresponds to "habbatin" (a grain).

It is stated: "For the purpose of Resurrection—meaning to bring reward and punishment into effect—We will set up the scales of justice, so on that day, no soul will be wronged in the least; rather, every soul will receive the exact reward of its deeds according to the scale. Even if someone's deed is equal to the weight of a mustard seed, We will bring it forward."

"Wa kafa bina hasibeen" (And sufficient are We as accountant)—meaning in this work, We do not need anyone's help or assistance; We will accomplish it alone. If anyone has imagined how We will be able to take this account of all creation, this is his delusion; and if anyone is in the delusion that God will be in need of obtaining some information regarding anyone from his assumed partners and intercessors, this too is merely a delusion.

In Surah Luqman, this same theme is stated in this manner, verse 16:
(O my son, indeed, if it should be the weight of a mustard seed and should be within a rock or [anywhere] in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth. Indeed, Allah is Subtle and Acquainted.)

The upcoming theme... Verses 48-77:
Above in verse 24, it was said through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Hadha dhikru man ma'iya wa dhikru man qabli"—meaning this same teaching which I am giving you was given by all the former prophets. Now, in an ascending order, reference is made to those great prophets who were the founders of nations. Above the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the possessor of Shariah prophet and messenger has been Hazrat Musa (Moses). [1]

Therefore, first their mention is made, and within their connection, Hazrat Haroon (Aaron) is also mentioned, who was their minister. Then above them, Hazrat Ibrahim (Abraham) is mentioned, who was the founder of nations and the leader of both lines of prophethood—Bani Israel and Bani Ismail—and within their connection, reference is made to Hazrat Ishaq (Isaac), Hazrat Ya'qoob (Jacob), and Hazrat Loot (Lot). Then above them, reference is made to Hazrat Nuh (Noah), who possesses the status of the second Adam. The reference to these great prophets is equivalent to the reference to the entire chain of guidance and direction, and the purpose of referencing them is to clarify before the opponents of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that the call which these prophets gave is the same call being given to you today; the characteristics possessed by those prophets are the same characteristics present in your Prophet too; and at the same time, it is reminded that the end met by those who denied those prophets will inevitably be your end too if you persist in denying him. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Verses 48-49:
Meaning of Furqan, Diya', and Dhikr:
"Furqan" refers to the criterion that distinguishes between truth and falsehood. Here, it refers to the Torah. Before the Quran, this status of the divine Book was held by it, and the divine Book is the very thing that serves as the criterion for distinguishing between truth and falsehood. Therefore, for this reason, the Quran is also called Furqan. "Diya'" means light. For the divine Book, terms like Diya' and Noor (light) have been used because it takes one out of all mental, moral, intellectual, and practical darknesses and guides toward the highway of guidance and recognition. "Dhikr" means reminder. Just as this word is used in places for the Quran, similarly here it is used for the Torah. We have pointed to its different aspects in the commentary of Surah Ta-Ha. Since in these scrolls, the realities of the horizons, souls, and history have been reminded, they are expressed as Dhikr.

It is stated: "We gave to Moses and Aaron the Book that distinguishes between truth and falsehood, and a light and reminder, but that Book became beneficial only for those God-fearing ones who, instead of believing only after seeing everything with their eyes, feared God and the Hereafter while in the unseen." The meaning is that similarly, this Book too will be effective only upon those whose light of intellect and nature is not extinguished; rather, they hold the fear of God and the Hereafter in their hearts. This theme has been discussed in detail under the initial verses of Surah Al-Baqarah; therefore, we content ourselves with a pointer here.

Meaning: "Just as We gave the Torah to Moses, similarly We have sent down this blessed Book too. Although it is entirely grace and blessing like beneficial rain, benefit will be derived from it only by those who possess capacity." "Afantum lahu munkiroon"—addressing the Quraysh, it is said: "Are you then denying this blessing?" The meaning is: if you are denying this blessing, then think over what you are denying!

The mention of Hazrat Musa has passed in detail in the previous Surah; therefore, making only a passing reference here, the mention of Hazrat Ibrahim is made forward in some detail.

The special status of Hazrat Ibrahim in guidance:
"Rushd" means guidance and recognition, and its annexation has created a special distinction in its meaning. That is, We gave to Abraham that guidance and recognition which was worthy of his rank and status. There are different stages of guidance and recognition. Whomever Allah grants a portion of it, He grants it according to his capacity, keeping in view his rank and status. The abundant portion Hazrat Ibrahim received of it, we have explained under the verse: (And when his Lord tried Abraham...) [Al-Baqarah: 124]. This special attribute of his is pointed out here.

"Wa kunna bihi 'alimeen"—meaning this high status We granted to him was not granted just like that, but after putting him through very severe trials, We tested him well and found him worthy of this status. Within this sentence, there is also a sarcasm on those who did not possess the courage to practice even the slightest Sunnah of Hazrat Ibrahim, yet claimed connection with him and on the strength of this connection, considered themselves entitled to the greatest status in both this world and the Hereafter. It is stated: "There is no room for this misplaced distribution in God's presence; Whomever He grants His recognition and wisdom, He grants keeping in view his capacity and courage."

Discussion with the nation regarding idols:
Now, an event of the very initial period of his life is being mentioned, from which his courage and chivalry are also clear, as well as that guidance and recognition which Allah granted him in his youth. Although his birth occurred in a polytheistic nation, an association-afflicted family, and the house of an idol-worshipping and idol-making father, Allah granted him that light of Tawheed from whose light the world is illuminated today and will remain illuminated until the Day of Resurrection. As soon as he gained consciousness, he called his father and his nation, saying: "What are these statues to which you are devoted?" The contempt and disregard for these idols in this phrase, and the sarcasm on the ignorance and rigidity of the father and nation in the word "'akifoon" (devoted), is not hidden from those who possess taste for the Arabic language. At this age and in such an environment, this slogan of truth can be raised only by one who is favored with special Divine success, and the majesty of whose faith is completely independent of every fear and greed.

Verses 53-54:
The practice of ancestors is not an argument in itself:
The greatest argument in favor of such foolishnesses has always been presented by their supporters: that their practice has been coming down from their ancestors. This very argument was presented by the people of Hazrat Ibrahim's nation, saying: "The worship of these idols was done by our ancestors, so how can we abandon them?" Although this statement is completely foolish, it is highly impressive for common people. A zeal and bias for the practice of ancestors takes root in hearts. But the light of recognition Allah had granted to Hazrat Ibrahim could not be extinguished by these blows. In reply, with complete determination and fearlessness, he said: "You and your ancestors have been in clear error, and no error will become guidance merely by the argument that it has been coming down from the time of ancestors; rather, it is necessary to test and verify it on the scale of intellect and nature!"

Verses 55-56:
Another step on the path of calling to truth:
By "Haqq" (truth) here is meant a serious and well-thought-out statement. This statement of Hazrat Ibrahim was so novel and strange for the people of his nation that they could not believe that someone could utter such a statement with serious thought. They assumed that this young man, in the passion of youth, without serious thought, has just said a word. Therefore, they asked: "Is what you are saying really your belief, or are you just playing a joke?" By declaring this slogan of truth of Hazrat Ibrahim as an unserious and careless slogan, they wanted to save people from its influence. But in reply, Hazrat Ibrahim took another step forward in calling to the truth. He said: "These stones and clay idols are not your Lord; rather, your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who created them, and I invite you to Him and proclaim Him among you." "Shahadah" (testimony) here is in the sense of calling and proclamation. In this sense, this word is used in places in the Quran.

The special method of argument of Hazrat Ibrahim:
Along with this, swearing an oath, Hazrat Ibrahim also informed the people of his intention: "When you turn away from here, I intend to carry out a secret plan against these idols." On the word "Kayd" (plan/plot), we have discussed in various places that it comes for any such action taken against an opponent in a way that he remains unaware of it.

From the words "tuwalloo mudbireen" (you turn away retreating), it appears that Hazrat Ibrahim gave this call inside the temple on the occasion of some assembly. Therefore, he said: "When you return to your homes from here, I intend to deal with your gods." Hazrat Ibrahim kept the actual scheme hidden. The form in which it manifested, its details are coming. But before reading this detail, take a look at those aspects of Hazrat Ibrahim's special method of discussion and argument, whose explanation we have done under verses 76-79 of Surah Al-An'am under the title "Some characteristics of Hazrat Ibrahim's method of argument". Just as Hazrat Ibrahim employs subtle and clean sarcasm in discussion and argument, similarly "istidraj" (gradual leading/entrapment) is also a highly prominent characteristic of Abrahamic argument. "Istidraj" means that in discussion, he brings his opponent to a move from where he does not even have a suspicion. Ultimately, he falls flat, and has to confess his defeat through his own tongue. This very method of "istidraj" was employed by Hazrat Ibrahim to convince his opponents on this occasion: he found an opportunity in the night and shattered all the small idols into pieces, leaving only the big idol, keeping in mind: "If the turn for questioning comes to me, I will say: 'This act seems to be that of this big chief, and instead of asking me, why do you not ask these oppressed ones themselves, if they can speak, they will tell their story of grief themselves, as to whose doing this calamity upon them is!'" It is obvious that after this, they will inevitably say: "They do not speak, so how can they be asked?" Then I will say to them: "O foolish ones! Those who cannot ward off a calamity from themselves, nor can they tell by whose hands this calamity came upon them, after all, what cure are they that you make them deities and bow down to them!" This is the mental scheme which Hazrat Ibrahim expressed as "Kayd". The aspects of sarcasm, mockery, and entrapment in it are obvious, and the manner in which the opponents had to helplessly bow down before this Abrahamic argument, its details are coming.

Execution of the idol-breaking scheme:
"Judhadh" refers to something shattered and broken into pieces. This is the description of the secret plan Hazrat Ibrahim adopted to bring his intention into effect. He found an opportunity and, leaving the big idol, shattered all the other idols into pieces. He left the big idol with the idea: "When the investigation of this matter takes place, I can say to these foolish people: 'This seems to be the doing of this big chief,' so that they may turn to him as to what this matter is; and if they say: 'They do not speak,' then the opportunity to complete the proof upon them will be obtained, showing: when these big and small are all mute, what is the meaning of making these mute and deaf deities?"

These are two separate independent sentences. From the first sentence, their surprise and worry are expressed, and from the second, their anger and wrath: meaning, when they saw this state of their deities, first the question arose among them: "After all, who can be the one who committed this audacity against our deities?" Then they expressed their anger and wrath: "Whoever did this act is indeed a great wrongdoer!"

Ultimately, those who were aware of Hazrat Ibrahim's inclinations, his above-mentioned call, and his challenge, said: "This can be the act only of that youth whom they call Ibrahim; we have heard him mentioning these idols with contempt." They also omitted the words of contempt in the same manner as they are omitted in verse 36. The eloquence of this omission we have explained there. They did not tolerate, as it were, quoting disbelief regarding their deities.
