The threat Hazrat Ibrahim had given to deal with the idols was given openly with an oath. It was not a hidden matter. His call of Tawheed was also completely open and manifest. Many people were aware of his statements, but until then, those who heard these statements of Hazrat Ibrahim, as passed above, used to dismiss them as his youthful passion and audacity. They did not assume that in fact Hazrat Ibrahim would carry out such an action as had now come before them; therefore, they neither felt the need to impose any restriction on him, nor made any arrangement for the protection of the idols. Their idea must have been: "No matter how detached someone may be from these deities of ours, who would dare to carry out a dangerous action against them!" But when this incident occurred, those who were acquainted with Hazrat Ibrahim said: "Be it as it may, this action is of that youth whom they call Ibrahim. We have heard him satirizing these deities."

From "yuqalu lahu ibraheem" (who is called Ibrahim), it is known that although Hazrat Ibrahim's call had fallen into many ears, he was not yet so well-known in the nation that people knew him personally; however, his name had reached various circles, and he was becoming known as a rebellious youth against the ancestral religion. On this basis, those giving testimony said in a contemptuous tone: "That same headstrong youth whom they call Ibrahim, Ibrahim, has been satirizing these deities of ours, so be it as it may, this is his doing!"

The trial of Hazrat Ibrahim:
After the accusation was established against Hazrat Ibrahim, the chiefs of the nation ordered: "Bring Hazrat Ibrahim before the common people in public, so that questioning may be done with him in the presence of the public, the entire matter comes before the people, and the people remain witnesses to it." This arrangement must have been considered necessary because this crime was, in their view, a severe crime whose punishment was also very harsh in their law; therefore, they wanted the investigation to take place before the public so that people might also estimate the recognition of the crime, and the punishment given to the criminal might also serve as a public lesson. It should be kept in mind that in that period, in such religious matters, the religious leaders were authorized to pass judgment and issue decrees. The government of the time (if any existed) merely executed their decree.

Verses 62-63:
The answer of Hazrat Ibrahim:
Ultimately, in accordance with the above-mentioned order, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was presented before the common people, in the court of the religious leaders, and they asked him: "Hazrat Ibrahim, have you committed this crime against our deities?" Hazrat Ibrahim answered in a highly sharp sarcasm and mockery: "This doing seems to be of this big chief of theirs, and what is the need to ask me about this? After all, why do you not ask them themselves, if they can speak, they will tell you their story themselves!"

Giving this answer, Hazrat Ibrahim brought the elders of his nation to that point where no way was left for them except the confession of defeat. They had to helplessly admit: "Laqad 'alimta ma ha'ula'i yantigoon" (You already know that these do not speak). After this confession of theirs, Hazrat Ibrahim said: "Uffun lakum wa lima ta'budoona min dooni Allahi afala ta'qiloon"—meaning: "If they are not even capable of telling you their own trouble, after all, what cure are they that you worship them? Fie upon you and upon these deities of yours. After all, why do you not use your intellect!"

Accusing Hazrat Ibrahim of fear or lying is based on ignorance of Arabic:
Those who have interpreted Hazrat Ibrahim's answer "bal fa'alahu kabeeruhum" (rather, this big one did it) as a lie or fear, did so due to ignorance of Arabic, failing to understand the eloquence of this statement. The question of fear does not arise because when he openly raises the call of Tawheed inside the temple of the nation, announces a secret plan against the idols, and then right in the court, before the entire nation, insults both the idols and their worshippers with the words "Uffun lakum", it is completely contrary to intellect to assume regarding such a man of truth that he will resort to verbal fabrication out of some fear.

As for its being a lie, apart from whether Hazrat Ibrahim can lie or not, there is no aspect in what was said that can be interpreted as a lie. It can be called a subtle sarcasm, a meaningful mockery, and a wise entrapment, but there is not the slightest trace of a lie in it. Look, even the opponents of Hazrat Ibrahim did not accuse him of lying, although if they had considered his answer a lie, they could have easily declared him a liar. Then, when his enemies did not dare to declare him a liar, why, God forbid, should we declare him a liar! [1]

Some were misled due to a Hadith in which it is mentioned that Hazrat Ibrahim spoke three lies, one of which is his statement: "bal fa'alahu kabeeruhum hadha". In our view, people have erred in understanding this Hadith. In Arabic, the word "kidhb" (lying/error) comes in various meanings. If it is said "kadhiba fulan", just as it can mean "he lied", it can also mean "he made a mistake" and can also mean "he employed double-meaning (Tawriyah)". Its correct meaning is determined by its context. The meaning of "Tawriyah" is to present one's statement before an opponent in such a way that the listener falls into a misconception. The entrapment we have mentioned above is also included in this. Highly subtle and clean examples of this "Tawriyah" are present in the Quran too, and in the statements of the righteous predecessors too. In the commentary of Surah Yusuf, some excellent examples have passed. A subtle and clean "Tawriyah" of Hazrat Ibrahim is also "Innee saqeem" (Indeed, I am sick). Its eloquence, if God wills, we will explain in the commentary of Surah As-Saffat. In it, not only is there no defect, but in certain situations against an enemy, it is an effective weapon of the truthful. In the Hadith, for the use of "kidhb" in this meaning for Hazrat Ibrahim, I can present testimonies from Arabic speech, but the discussion will go to another corner; therefore, I postpone it for some appropriate occasion.

A clear proof of the refutation of Shirk:
It should also be kept in mind that the statement of our master Hazrat Ibrahim, "bal fa'alahu kabeeruhum hadha", is in itself a highly clear proof of the refutation of Shirk, which is stated in different styles in the Quran. If different small and big deities are assumed in this universe, there is no reason why the Greatest God should be pleased with accepting the partnership of others in His godship; and even if, hypothetically, due to some interest He were pleased at some time, it is not far-fetched that one day He might become angry and break all of them into pieces. This very reality was demonstrated by our master Hazrat Ibrahim through his action, and his opponents also realized this reality, as will be clear from the upcoming verse, though they could not remain firm on it. In any case, in our view, this is a highly exalted, wise entrapment and argument, and a disgrace and mockery of Shirk and the people of Shirk. There is no slight reason to interpret it as a lie.

One step forward, then backwards:
That is, this blow of Hazrat Ibrahim warned them, and they turned to the voice of their conscience and the guidance of their intellect and heart, saying to one another: "You call this youth a wrongdoer, but he has proved that the wrongdoer is not he, but the wrongdoers are you yourselves who make such things deities which do not even possess the capability to ward off a calamity that has come upon their own heads, or even state it to someone else."

"Naks" means to turn something upside down in such a way that its feet are up and its head is down. By this warning of Hazrat Ibrahim, they did open their eyes for a moment, but then they became upside down, saying: "You already know that these do not speak, so how can we ask them!"... Those who are involved in such ignorance, sometimes a light falls on their hearts through some event or warning in such a way that they begin to see the straight path clearly, but the zeal of ignorance is not something that leaves easily; therefore, they become blind and deaf again, and taking one correct step, they adopt the same reverse path they were walking.

The full blow of Hazrat Ibrahim:
Hazrat Ibrahim brought his opponents to the very point where he wanted to bring them. They cried out: "They do not speak, so how can they be asked about their state!" After this confession of theirs, Hazrat Ibrahim struck them with a full blow. He said: "Then why do you worship besides Allah such things which can neither benefit you nor harm you!" This discourse, as is obvious, after exposing the helplessness of the idols in this manner, could be as effective as it could not have been without it. Without it, if this same matter had been brought before them, they could have made excuses in reply, but now when they were lying shattered, telling the story of their insignificance and helplessness through the language of their state, what could their worshippers present in their support?

"Uffun lakum" is a word of highly severe dislike and aversion. When Hazrat Ibrahim completed the proof upon them in this manner, and still their eyes did not open, he said with severe aversion and detachment: "Fie upon you and upon these deities of yours whom you worship besides Allah! Afala ta'qiloon—meaning, are you completely like senseless beasts! What is obtained from worshipping these stone statues whose helplessness is such that they cannot protect themselves, so how will they protect you!"

The sentence of punishment for Hazrat Ibrahim from the religious court:
When the worshippers and the priests of the temple saw that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had put their entire business of leadership in danger, and no honor was left for them or their deities, they instigated their common folk: "This is the time to rise in support of your deities. If some harsh action is not taken against this youth at this time, the religion of ancestors will be destroyed." It appears that this appeal had an effect on the common people, and some proposals regarding action against Hazrat Ibrahim also came forward, but the priests were not satisfied with those proposals. They urged: "If you really want to take an effective action against this man, then do this: burn him." The priests wanted to completely eradicate this trial by giving this punishment, according to their assumption.

The fire became cool and safe for Hazrat Ibrahim:
According to this decision, it appears that an attempt was made to burn Hazrat Ibrahim in fire, but Allah made this fire cool and safe for Hazrat Ibrahim. What form did this take? There is no specification of this in the Quran, but there is no improbability in this matter. The characteristics and effects of things are entirely subject to the command of Allah. He may turn whichever poison He wills into an antidote for whomsoever He wills, and whichever antidote He wills into poison for whomsoever He wills. Such wondrous manifestations of power we observe even today. If God, for the protection of one of His prophets, turned the fire into a garden, what strange thing is this!

Failure of the priests' plot and Hazrat Ibrahim's migration:
The priests made a plot to burn Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in fire, to which pointer is made in verse 98 of Surah As-Saffat. If God wills, we will explain it there. This plot of the priests failed, and after this, Hazrat Ibrahim migrated from that region.

By "al-arda allatee barakna feeha lil-'alameen" (the land which We blessed for the worlds) is meant a fertile and fresh region. This is a pointer to the land of Canaan. That is, when the situation reached the point that it became impossible for Hazrat Ibrahim to stay in his homeland, We saved him and Lot from the evil of their nation and gave them shelter in the region of Canaan. Hazrat Lot was the nephew of Hazrat Ibrahim. Although the sphere of call of both these gentlemen was separate, Hazrat Lot was also with him in this migration. It appears that until this migration, both gave the call to their nation, but after the migration, they made different regions their centers. It is not known what deal was made with the nation of Hazrat Ibrahim after the migration.

Verses 72-73:
Allah's favor upon Hazrat Ibrahim:
This is the favor Allah described which He did upon Hazrat Ibrahim one after another after the migration. Hazrat Ibrahim had abandoned his nation and relatives for the sake of Allah, so Allah, by His grace, granted him sons and grandsons. Those whom he had abandoned were wicked and insolent, and those whom Allah gave him in their stead were all among the righteous and the chosen. By "Nafilah" (an additional gift), the expression of the reality is intended that Hazrat Ibrahim obtained Hazrat Ismail in reply to his prayer, and additionally, Allah also granted him Isaac and Jacob, and after that, a chain of prophethood was established. [1] In "wa ja'alnahum...", pointer is made to the fact that they became the founders of nations and the leaders of communities. Although every prophet, by virtue of his office, is a leader, some prophets have passed under whose shadow no one stood but themselves; however, Hazrat Isaac and Hazrat Jacob (peace be upon them) were elevated to the office of leadership by the grace of Allah, and they led a great nation.

"Wa awhayna ilayhim fi'la al-khayrati wa iqama as-salati wa eeta'a az-zakati wa kanoo lana 'abideen"—"iqama as-salati" is actually "iqamata as-salati"; the 'ta' of "iqamah" fell away due to the demand of rhythm and sound. This kind of abbreviation is well-known in Arabic in keeping with the demand of sound. By counting these parts of the call of these prophets, the purpose is to show that their call was the same as the Quran is giving, and all these prophets were worshippers of God alone, they did not bow down before anyone other than the One God. The meaning is: those who claim the name of these prophets and connection with them should look at their faces in this mirror and review their speech and action—what they were, and what these have become.

Verses 74-75:
The favor of judgment and knowledge upon Hazrat Lot:
Above, the mention of Hazrat Lot came in connection with Hazrat Ibrahim. Now, their independent mention is made: that We granted them also judgment and knowledge. By "Hukm" (judgment) is meant the power and capability of decision, and by "'Ilm" (knowledge) is meant the knowledge of prophethood. The first thing is the fruit of the purity of intellect and nature; the second thing is the blessing of divine revelation. Those whom Allah chose for the office of prophethood were the best fruits of the tree of nature. They nurtured their natural capabilities until their inner self was illuminated with the light of wisdom, becoming capable of acquiring the light of revelation. Then, when Allah favored them with the light of revelation, they became light upon light.

Migration of Hazrat Lot:
"Wa najjaynahu mina al-qaryati allatee kanat ta'malu al-khaba'ith"—the form of their salvation has been pointed out: that along with Hazrat Ibrahim, they too migrated toward the land of Syria. Since the punishment of Allah came upon the people of their town, their departure from there is expressed as salvation. "Kanat ta'malu al-khaba'ith"...

[1] Under verses 100-112 of Surah As-Saffat, if God wills, this discussion will come: that Hazrat Ibrahim obtained Hazrat Ismail in reply to his prayer, and Hazrat Isaac in reward for the sacrifice of Hazrat Ismail. This historical reality has been distorted by the Jews, the details of which can be seen in the treatise 'Dhabeeh' by the teacher-imam.

...points to the wickedness of homosexuality, which in some places is expressed as "fahishah" (indecency), but the plural word "khaba'ith" points to the reality that this type of evil never exists alone; rather, when it is born in a nation, an entire family of indecencies and wickednesses settles under its shadow. No detail of these evils is given here because such disgusting evils are not worthy of mention.

The end of the people of Lot:
"Innahum kanoo qawma saw'in fasiqeen"—this is the reason stated as to why Allah took His servant out from among those people and sent down His punishment. It is stated: "They were wicked and disobedient people, and Lot was righteous and well-doing." Allah took His righteous servant out from among those insolent people and buried those wicked ones. In "wa adkhalnahu fee rahmatina" (and We admitted him into Our mercy), by mercy is meant both that salvation and success which they obtained in this world and that victory and prosperity which they will obtain in the Hereafter.

The reference to Hazrat Nuh:
The accusative case of "Noohan" is born from the meaning understood here from the connection of speech. That is, just as We sent down Our mercy upon Our above-mentioned servants, saved them, and helped them, similarly before that, We also saved Our servant Noah and helped him.

The final prayer of Hazrat Nuh:
"Idh nada min qablu fastajabna lahu"—this is a pointer to that final prayer of Hazrat Noah which is mentioned in detail in Surah Nuh, reading which the heart trembles. The form in which the acceptance of this prayer manifested, its details will come in Surah Nuh. Here, only remember the final verses of that connection:
(Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire, and they found not for themselves besides Allah any helpers. And Noah said, "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant. Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except the wicked, one who is ungrateful. My Lord, forgive me and my parents and whoever enters my house a believer and the believing men and believing women. And do not increase the wrongdoers except in ruin.") [Nuh: 25-28]

"Fanajjaynahu wa ahlahu mina al-karbi al-'azeem"—the research on the word "Ahl" has passed under verse 132 of Surah Ta-Ha. It refers to a person's household, followers, and companions. That is, Allah saved him and his believing companions from a great distress and a very big calamity. By "karb 'azeem" (great distress) is meant both that grief and pain which afflicted him and his oppressed companions for a long period due to the oppressions and tortures of the enemies of truth, and that great punishment in which his entire nation was ultimately involved.

The end of the people of Nuh:
After "Nasarnahu", the preposition "Min" is a clue that here this word contains the meaning of "Intaqamna" (We took revenge). We have been explaining this style of Arabic in various places. Therefore, in our view, its translation will be: "We helped him and We took revenge upon those who denied Our signs." We have tried to open this implication in the translation. In "innahum kanoo qawma saw'in" (indeed, they were a wicked people), pointer is made in brief to their crime: that they were highly wicked people. The details of their crimes have passed in the previous surahs too, and will come forward in Surah Nuh too. Here, only remember this much: that the long period for which Hazrat Noah shook his nation, perhaps no other example of it can be found easily; and the state of Hazrat Noah's sympathy and grief has been such as will come in detail from the tongue of Hazrat Noah himself in the commentary of Surah Nuh, yet they were such stone-like people that nothing was effective upon them. "Fa-aghraqnahum ajma'een"—the punishment of this hardheartedness and wickedness of theirs was that Allah drowned the entire nation. Rather, this entire nation was a heap of filth on God's earth, to remove which Allah commanded the oceans of the earth and the clouds of the sky to wash this land of God clean from this impurity. Consequently, this is a matter of fact: the Deluge of Noah washed this land clean, and Allah populated it anew from Hazrat Noah and his pure-natured companions. To open this theme well, what has come in the Torah regarding the people of Noah is highly useful. But the appropriate place for it, if God wills, will come in the commentary of Surah Nuh.

The upcoming theme... Verses 78-94:
In the previous verses, in a historical order, those great prophets were mentioned who were the founders of creeds and nations. Now forward, in an attributive order, those prophets are mentioned who, in terms of their attributes and character, are models for the entire humanity. In this connection, first Hazrat Dawud (David) and Hazrat Sulayman (Solomon) are mentioned, who were the best examples of the blend of kingship and asceticism. Allah granted them worldly power, but instead of falling into its trials, they remained constantly grateful to God, and spent all their capabilities and resources in seeking the pleasure of their Lord alone. After that, Hazrat Ayub (Job), Hazrat Ismail, Hazrat Idrees (Enoch), and Hazrat Dhul-Kifl are mentioned, who, in terms of their patience, are the chosen ones of humanity. Allah put them through great trials, and in every trial they remained steadfast. After that, Hazrat Yunus (Jonah), Hazrat Zakariyya (Zechariah), and Hazrat Maryam (Mary) are mentioned, who called upon their Lord in highly dark and hopeless situations. Allah accepted their prayer, and made the impossible possible for them through His special mercy. In the lives of these prophets, there is a lesson both for those arrogant ones who, on obtaining God's blessings, were arrogant against God, and for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his oppressed companions who were passing through a highly delicate period of trial, and before whom the path was seen completely closed. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Verses 78-79:
"Dawuda wa Sulaymana" are in the accusative case here in the same manner as "Noohan" was in the accusative case above—meaning, in the light of the context, we assume some omitted verb. We have opened this omitted verb in the translation.

"Nafashat" means the grazing of sheep or cattle in the night, and the feminine pronoun in "fafahhamnaha" refers to that decision which is understood and implied from the context. Examples of this have passed before.

Pointer to a lawsuit of Hazrat Dawud, and the purpose of this pointer is the dispensation of justice by Dawud and Sulayman:
Here, pointer is made to a lawsuit which was presented in the court of Hazrat Dawud. No detail of this lawsuit is given. Only this much is known: that someone's flock of sheep had fallen into someone's field. Hazrat Dawud passed a decision on it, but in this decision, he could not reach the depth of the matter. His son Hazrat Sulayman, who was still young, presented his opinion which was more correct and closer to justice. Ultimately, Hazrat Dawud passed the decision according to his opinion. The question as to what decision the father passed and what opinion the son gave is out of discussion, because the purpose of the Quran here is not to present the proceedings of the lawsuit, but, first, to show that Hazrat Dawud, despite being a ruler and a prophet, was so cautious in his decisions that when the weakness of one of his independent judgments became clear to him through the drawing of attention by his young son, he immediately changed his decision and accepted the opinion of one junior to him; second, to show that Allah granted Hazrat Dawud such a son who, from his youth, possessed such a high power of decision that sometimes he gave advice to his great father, and the father appreciated his decisions. This is a very great fortune of a father to be granted such a capable son who not only becomes his hand and support in the affairs of government but also sometimes guides him. To clarify this reality, pointer is made to this lawsuit here. The details of this lawsuit presented by our commentators are completely external to the Quran. Different forms of it can be assumed, but their nature is entirely hypothetical, and we do not like to pursue hypotheses.

The true jewel of a ruler:
"Wa kunna lihukmihim shahideen"—meaning, We did not sit detached after giving government to the people of this family in Our land, but kept observing what they do, how much sense of establishing peace and justice among the subjects they possess, and how wise, cautious, selfless, and impartial they are in the matter of their responsibilities. Consequently, this father and son proved that both are fully worthy of this office. As soon as the son felt that the father had erred in the matter of justice, the father bowed down before the truth without any hesitation. This character is the true jewel of governance, and Allah gives succession in His land to whomsoever He gives only to try this jewel.

"Wa kullan atayna hukman wa 'ilman"—meaning, this is their jewel on the basis of which We favored each of them with wisdom and prophethood.

Asceticism alongside governance:
"Wa sakharna ma'a Dawuda al-jibala yusabbihna wat-tayr"—this is a pointer to the asceticism of Hazrat Dawud: that on one hand We had seated them on the throne of government, and they established peace and justice among the subjects with complete alertness and selflessness; on the other hand, their connection with Allah was such that they would go out into the mountains in the night, and the echo of their songs and hymns of glorification would resound in the mountains, and the birds too would join them. It should be kept in mind that from the Torah, this fact is proved: Hazrat Dawud was highly melodious, and along with this melody, he possessed pain and burning. Furthermore, all his prayers are in the form of songs and hymns, and these songs are inspired. The state of these songs is such that if you read the Psalms, although in translation their poetic spirit has departed, yet reading them even today, it is felt that the heart will leap out of the chest. When a melodious and pain-filled person like Hazrat Dawud read these inspired songs in the lap of the mountains at the pleasant time of dawn, surely their echo must have been heard from the mountains, and the birds too must have joined him. Do not think of this as mere poetic imagination; rather, it is a reality. Every single thing in this universe, as is specified in the Quran, glorifies its Lord, but we do not understand their glorification. Their desire for glorification burns even more when some pain-filled person strikes a song which translates their heart's desire; at that time, they too sway and join their tune with his tune. If we do not hear or understand the glorification of mountains and birds, do not think that no one else hears or understands it. Those hear and understand it in whose chests is a melted heart. Maulana Rumi has said well:
"The philosopher is a denier of the speaking pillar (Hannanah),
He is a stranger to the senses of the prophets."

To this reality, Mirza Ghalib has pointed thus:
"You alone are not confidant to the voices of secret,
Otherwise, here, whatever veil exists is the curtain of the instrument."

The style of expression of this segment is also worthy of consideration. It is not said "sakharna lahu" (We subjected for him) but, changing the style, "sakharna ma'ahu" (We subjected with him). From "ma'ahu", the meaning is born that in the glorification of Hazrat Dawud, Allah made the mountains and birds also his companions of the assembly. When he struck his song of praise, they too joined him.

A point of language:
Here, between the conjoined and conjoined-to—meaning "jibal" (mountains) and "tayr" (birds)—the verb "yusabbihna" (they glorify) is intervening, whereas apparently it should have come after "tayr". The reason for this advancement of the verb is that the glorification of birds was a well-known matter, while the song-singing of mountains was a rare matter. For this reason, the verb was conjoined with them.

A God-made matter:
"Wa kunna fa'ileen"—meaning, these works are not for anyone else to do but for Us alone to do, and We alone became their doers. We may set trees, stones, rivers, mountains, and beasts and birds after whomsoever We will. These blessings of God are not obtained by anyone through their own force and power, but are obtained only by God's giving.

Ascetic of the night and knight of the day:
That is, do not think of this night-awakening ascetic as merely one who sat in the corners of solitude doing 'Allah Hu'; rather, just as he was an ascetic of the night, he was a knight of the day too. We taught him to make a specific garment for protection from the attacks of battle. By this specific garment, pointer is made to the coat of mail. Its inventor is Hazrat Dawud. It is proved from history that the high types of coats of mail invented by Hazrat Dawud did not exist before him. In the martial poetry of Arabic, the mention of Dawudi mail-coats comes very often. In the defensive weaponry of that era, the greatest importance was held by this. Now the times have changed. These instruments and weapons do not hold any importance now. But the era of which this is a matter, in that era, Hazrat Dawud was the master of the greatest military power of the world due to these inventions of his. The purpose of its mention is that Hazrat Dawud was the true picture of 'ascetic by night and knight by day'. On one hand, the heart of mountains would melt with his pain-filled prayers and supplications; on the other hand, enemies would tremble with his martial inventions and military campaigns.

Invitation to follow Hazrat Dawud in gratitude:
"Fahal antum shakiroon"—after highlighting both these aspects of Hazrat Dawud's life, addressing the listeners, it is asked: "Do you too possess the courage to become grateful servants of this kind?" Despite obtaining wisdom, prophethood, government, and strength, such was the state of Hazrat Dawud's poverty and asceticism. This very blend of religion and world is desired by God, and God's grateful servants are like this, not those greedy ones who sing the songs of Dawud and Sulayman's majesty but have become dogs of the world.

The great naval fleet of Hazrat Sulayman:
Here, "Li" is a clue that the verb is omitted. The meaning of "sakharna lahu" we have explained in its place: that with the preposition "Li", this word comes in the meaning of setting something in the service of someone. That is, We had subjected the fierce winds also and set them in the service of Solomon. It is known from histories that the land power of Hazrat Dawud was indeed very great, but the naval power of their government developed peerlessly in the era of Hazrat Sulayman. He invented such sailing ships which traveled to India and western islands. His naval fleet was the most powerful fleet of the time. In the Red Sea, its center was Tarshish, located in the Gulf of Aqaba, and in the Mediterranean, the ports of Tyre, Sidon, and Jaffa. Until that era, the medium of controlling sea winds was sailing. These sails served as engines for the ships. Hazrat Sulayman invented such sails which did not let the movement of ships be disrupted even in fierce winds. They continued their long journeys in their determined directions, in every kind of favorable and unfavorable winds, without any fear. Since this thing was the fruit of the science taught by God, it is stated: "We subjected for Solomon the fierce winds which ran by his command."

By "al-arda allatee barakna feeha" (the land which We blessed), pointer is made to Palestine, Syria, etc., which, due to being fertile, held great importance from a commercial point of view.

"Wa kunna bikulli shay'in 'alimeen"—the exact same sentence has entered in verse 51 in the honor of Hazrat Ibrahim with a minor change. There we have explained its meaning. The meaning is: We knew well the capabilities, capacity, and courage of Hazrat Sulayman. For this reason, We granted him this control over Our winds, and We did not sit closing Our eyes after giving all this, but kept knowing and observing how Solomon used the power granted by Us. In fact, this is the secret which has not entered the understanding of the scientists of our era.

Conquest of the universe is the favor of God:
In the successes they have obtained and are obtaining in the conquest of the universe, instead of considering them the favor of God, they assume them the miracle of their own intelligence, whereas whatever success man obtains, he obtains through the intellect granted by God and God's guidance alone. To this reality, pointer is made above by "wa kunna fa'ileen". Then, when man falls into this delusion that all these inventions are his own achievement, another delusion is born from the womb of this delusion which is even more fatal—meaning, he begins to think that he may work with these acquired powers as he wishes, no one is there to hold his hand. The Quran has struck a blow at this delusion also by "wa kunna bikulli shay'in 'alimeen": showing that power is not foolish to hand over the keys of its treasures to people and sit detached, letting people make whatever they wish; rather, it is aware of everything, and when it is aware, it means that if anyone used the power granted by it wrongly, he will inevitably be accountable for it one day. Hazrat Dawud and Hazrat Sulayman (peace be upon them) were well-aware of this secret; therefore, despite obtaining great successes in both land and sea, not for a single moment did they fall into this delusion that these were their own achievements; rather, they considered all these things the favor of God, and whatever steps they took, they took thinking that one day they have to be presented before God and answer for their every action. This is the very feeling which makes man a true grateful and obedient servant of God. This aspect of Hazrat Dawud and Sulayman's (peace be upon them) lives, if God wills, will come under discussion in detail in the commentary of Surah An-Naml.

Hazrat Sulayman used corrupt elements in construction and civilization:
By "shayeeteen" (devils) is meant those devils of jinn whom Hazrat Sulayman had subjected. It is proved from both the Quran and the Torah that he possessed regular armies of humans, jinn, and birds, whose parade took place. For example, He said: (And gathered for Solomon were his soldiers of the jinn and men and birds, and they were [kept] in rows.) [An-Naml: 17]. In Surah An-Naml, there is also the mention of an Ifrit jinn of Hazrat Sulayman's army who said to him: "If the presence commands, I will bring the throne of the Queen of Sheba before you arise from your place." These jinn also dived in the seas to provide sea wealth, pearls, etc., and did other works. Subjecting such corrupt elements and using them in the useful service of humanity is a great achievement of Hazrat Sulayman (peace be upon him), and the true work of a capable ruler is this: that he should set not only useful elements but also harmful elements, by his knowledge and wisdom, on the path of construction, civilization, reform, and progress. On the knowledge granted to Hazrat Sulayman to subject these devils, a detailed discussion has passed under verse 102 of Al-Baqarah in connection with Harut and Marut. Some have interpreted "shayateen" as those disbelieving nations who were under Hazrat Sulayman's control, but in our view, this is a kind of deviation from this word. There is no doubt that in the Quran, this word is used for both the wicked of jinn and the wicked of humans, but for absolute disbelieving nations, this word has not been used anywhere; and if it is said that by this is meant the wicked of the disbelieving nations, then there is room for this within the word, but not principally, rather followingly; principally, it refers to the wicked of the jinn.

A delusion of the scientists of our era:
"Wa kunna lahum hafideen"—meaning, Our subjecting these satanic powers for Hazrat Sulayman was not that they were under his control merely on the strength of his own force; rather, their true guardian was We. We, by Our favor, had put them under Solomon's control, and We alone were their guardian lest any of them flee or commit any transgression against Hazrat Sulayman's intention. If We were not their guardian, they could have become a cause of danger and calamity by becoming uncontrolled. Within this segment, there is a very great warning for those who, after conquering some powers of nature, assume that now they are their masters, neither can anyone snatch them from them nor can they commit any rebellion against them. This idea is merely the result of man's narrow capacity and ignorance. Today, man has put the powers of steam, gas, electricity, and atom under his control, but God alone has given man control over them, and they are under man's control only until God wills. If Allah were to loosen the rein of even one of these powers, the very powers with whose help man is joining the heaven and the earth would burn all his harvest in the blink of an eye.

Hazrat Ayub and his exemplary patience:
After presenting two high examples of gratitude, now comes the mention of those prophets who established highly exalted examples of patience. In this connection, first Hazrat Ayub (Job) is mentioned. From the Book of Job, it is known that in the land of Uz, Hazrat Ayub was a highly perfect and upright man. God had granted him a great family and great wealth. He had seven sons and three daughters. Seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, one thousand oxen, and five hundred donkeys of burden were with him. His servants and maids were also countless. Among the people of the East, there was no other wealthy person of this rank, yet despite possessing all this service and pomp, he was a highly grateful and obedient servant of God, never defiled by any evil.

On this righteousness of his, Satan and his agents became jealous. They gibed: "This fear of God and uprightness of Job is because God has given him every kind of prosperity. If he were deprived of them, he would never be a grateful servant of God." [1]

After this, a chain of trials and calamities from God began for him. His cattle were looted, servants were killed, children died, and wealth and pomp all disappeared, but instead of becoming hopeless of God, Hazrat Ayub fell at His door. In the Book of Job, it is: "he fell upon the ground, and worshipped, and said, Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return thither: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord." Book of Job 1:20-21

After financial calamities and distresses, the chain of physical suffering and pain began, and the situation reached the point: "so went Satan forth from the presence of the Lord, and smote Job with sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown. And he took him a potsherd to scrape himself withal; and he sat down among the ashes." Book of Job 2:7-8

Hazrat Ayub faced these sufferings too with the same patience and pleasure with which he faced financial calamities and distresses. Ultimately, he arose from this trial too with goodness and beauty, in reward for which Allah granted him more than what he had lost.

"And the Lord turned the captivity of Job... also the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before. Then came there unto him all his brethren, and all his sisters... So the Lord blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning... He had also seven sons and three daughters... After this lived Job an hundred and forty years, and saw his sons, and his sons' sons, even four generations." Book of Job 42:10-16

The station of pleasure and its reward:
"Annee massaniya ad-durru wa anta arhamu ar-rahimeen"—keep in view this eloquence of this prayer: Hazrat Dawud referred to his pain and grief, but did not bring the word of demand on his tongue. He left it to the mercy of God. He said: "I am afflicted, and You are the most merciful of the merciful." The meaning is: I present my pain before You, and refer the matter to Your mercy; whatever You will do, You will do according to Your status of mercy, and in that alone is blessing and mercy for me. This is the very station which is called the station of pleasure (rida); whoever achieves this station, for him alone in the Hereafter is the rank of "pleased and pleasing". In this world, the situations and trials through which man has to pass, to determine in which of them lies his good in terms of both this world and the Hereafter is highly difficult for man. Sometimes, what he desires, his success and prosperity in terms of its end lies in its opposite; therefore, in all those matters in which the aspect of good is not determined from God, referring the matter to Allah alone is prosperity. He alone knows whether sickness is better for the servant or health, death is better or life!

"Fastajabna lahu fakashafna ma bihi min durrin wa ataynahu ahlahu wa mithlahum ma'ahum"—Allah accepted Hazrat Ayub's prayer. He removed all those sufferings of his in which he was involved, and granted him his family and companions again anew. It should be kept in mind that Hazrat Ayub, in his prayer, pointed only to his suffering, and did not express any desire for family and wealth. In the situations in which he was involved, for him, this alone was a big thing that his pain be removed—where was the room to desire more than this! But Allah, by His favor, granted him what he asked and also what he, due to his humility, was not considering himself worthy of.

From "wa ataynahu ahlahu wa mithlahum ma'ahum" (and We gave him his family and the like of them with them), the point emerges that in this period of trial, his family and relatives who had scattered, Allah gathered them again, gave the like of those who had died in their stead, and further expanded the circle of his relatives.

"Rahmatan min 'indina wa dhikra lil-'abideen"—meaning, this which happened occurred by Our special favor. The situations apparently were completely hopeless, neither could Job expect it nor anyone else, but We, by Our mercy, did everything for Our servant Job. "Wa dhikra lil-'abideen"—meaning, We did this also so that this event might remain a memorial for Our worshipping servants: that Our servants who remain associated with Us in every kind of soft and hard situations, We favor them in this manner and will favor them.

Ismail, Idrees, and Dhul-Kifl (peace be upon them).

Verses 85-86:
On Hazrat Ismail and Hazrat Idrees (peace be upon them), discussion has passed under verses 54-57 of Surah Maryam. The attributive similarity between these two prophets we have also pointed out there. Here, along with them, Hazrat Dhul-Kifl is also included, and regarding all these three gentlemen, it is said: "Kullun mina as-sabireen" (All were of the patient), from which it is known that the attribute of patience is a prominent characteristic of all these three gentlemen. Among them, the patience of Hazrat Ismail is clearly known—the Quran mentions their patience in places—but the life story of Hazrat Idrees and Hazrat Dhul-Kifl is completely in the veil of obscurity. In the ancient scrolls, their mention is not present by these names. Now, either it has happened that in the Arabic accent, these names became completely changed, or their names disappeared from the ancient scrolls and only the Quran revived them anew. Whichever form occurred, regarding both these prophets, in my humble knowledge, there is no noteworthy thing. Some have applied Dhul-Kifl to Ezekiel, but this is reliable only when it is proved that Ezekiel was titled with this title. The names of both these prophets became introduced only through the Quran, and patience is stated as their prominent characteristic. It should be kept in mind that neither in the Torah nor in the Quran are the names and situations of all the prophets mentioned; the knowledge of all the prophets is with Allah alone.

The nature of the slip of Dhul-Noon (peace be upon him):
By Dhul-Noon is meant Hazrat Yunus (Jonah). In the ancient scrolls, his name has come as Jonah. Here, Allah has mentioned him with the title of Dhul-Noon (Companion of the Fish). "Noon" means fish; since the fish had swallowed him, he was given this title, which contains a reference to the event of the fish and also a kind of love.

Hazrat Yunus was sent as a messenger to the people of Nineveh. The population of Nineveh, according to the narration of the Book of Jonah, was one hundred and twenty thousand. In Surah As-Saffat of the Quran too, the words "mi'ata alfin aw yazeedoon" (one hundred thousand or more) have entered, which supports this narration. Hazrat Yunus called the people of Nineveh for a long period, but no one listened to his word. Ultimately, the zeal for truth overcame him, and becoming hopeless and aggrieved by this disregard and hardheartedness of the nation, he stood up from there. He thought: "When putting a leech on these stones is not possible, why waste one's time behind such ungrateful ones and keep oneself in trouble for their sake!" For a person of truth, the birth of this feeling inside him on the disregard of truth is a natural thing, but in the dominance of this feeling, a highly important aspect of the matter became out of Hazrat Yunus's sight—meaning, when should a messenger of God abandon his nation? This is not a matter for him to decide on his own, but is for God alone to decide. God alone knows when the cup of any nation becomes full, and when it is permissible for a messenger to declare detachment from his nation, migrate, and hand over the nation to the punishment of God. For the prophets, this has always been the Divine Sunnah: that they should not abandon their nation without the permission of God, even if saws run on their heads, and even if not a single person is found to listen to their call. Hazrat Yunus, in the passion of zeal for truth, could not keep this Divine Sunnah in view. He thought: "After trying to awaken and shake the people for a long period, when no one has come to listen to the word, now before God I am free of responsibility; I should abandon such an insolent nation to God's punishment and detach myself from them." To consider himself free of responsibility also meant that he did not expect any grasp from God on this action of his; rather, his assumption was that in this manner, he would escape from this trial in which he was placed.

This idea of Hazrat Yunus was based on a pure feeling, but the trials Allah has prescribed for His servants must inevitably be completed. Becoming averse to the nation, he boarded a ship which stood fully ready to depart on a journey. After covering some distance, the ship was surrounded by a storm. When the storm did not seem to subside, the sailors, according to the common superstition of that era, thought: "Be it as it may, some slave who has fled from his master has boarded this ship; until he is caught and handed over to the sea, salvation from this storm cannot be obtained." Ultimately, to find out who the runaway slave in the ship was, lots were cast, and the duty of casting lots, as is pointed out in Surah As-Saffat, was handed over to Hazrat Yunus himself. The reason for this probably was that among all the people of the ship, he appeared to be the most trustworthy man. In any case, lots were cast, and the lot came out in Hazrat Yunus's name, as a result of which he was thrown from the ship into the sea. In the sea, a big fish, probably a whale, swallowed him, but to digest a messenger of God is not easy. When Hazrat Yunus saw that the path he adopted to escape from the darkness of difficulties had put him into even deeper darknesses, that immortal prayer arose from his heart, for which there is a guarantee: if it is done with a sincere heart, it is never deprived of acceptance. Allah accepted his prayer, and the fish vomited him onto the shore. There, to protect his exhausted body from the heat and intensity of the sun, Allah made a gourd or some similar vine grow under which he obtained shelter. When his senses returned from this incident and some life came into his body, he was directed to go again to the people of Nineveh for warning. From the Book of Jonah, it is known that for this second warning, power also created an effective medium—meaning, that vine whose shade had given security to Hazrat Yunus dried up suddenly for some reason. Grateful people appreciate the favor of even small things. Hazrat Yunus felt grieved by this drying up of the memorial vine. Allah drew his attention to this: "A insignificant vine, in whose planting and nurturing you did not put any labor, you became so grieved on its drying up—so consider: how can I hand over this great Nineveh to My punishment, whom I created, nourished, and nurtured! Go call them again; perhaps they will adopt the path of goodness and become worthy of My mercy!" In accordance with this direction, Hazrat Yunus went again to the people of Nineveh for warning, and this second call of his had such an effect that from the king to the common citizens of Nineveh, all trembled, all believed in God, the king took off his royal robes and put on a garment of sackcloth, and issued a decree in the name of the citizens: "Every one should turn back from his evil way, keep fast, cry before God, and bow the head in repentance!"

Since in the narration of Hazrat Yunus's event, commentators have made a big mess, I have presented its details to clarify its correct nature. This detail is based on the Book of Jonah and the indications of the Quran. Forward in the commentary of Surah As-Saffat, if God wills, its further explanation will come. Keeping this detail in view, now ponder over the parts of the verse.

Anger on the disregard of the nation:
"Idh dhahaba mughadiban"—the object of "mughadiban" is omitted here based on context. That is, becoming angry and aggrieved by his nation, he abandoned them and departed. This grief and anger, as is obvious, could be on this: he tried all means to warn them, but they did not care in the least; rather, they insulted the truth and mocked his words.

An implicit aspect of Hazrat Yunus's action:
"Fadhanna an lan naqdira 'alayhi"—meaning, he thought that in this manner, he would escape from this calamity in which he was currently placed by the hands of the nation. In this passion of zeal, his attention did not go to this matter: that on the front on which God has appointed him, if he abandons it without God's command, God can put him into an even bigger trial. Within this sentence, Allah has reflected on such an aspect of his action which, although was not in Hazrat Yunus's sight, was implied in it. Hazrat Yunus could not imagine for a single moment that if he abandoned the nation and departed, he would go out of God's grasp. However, this assumption must have occurred to him: "By separating from these insolent ones, I will escape from this trouble in which I am currently involved for their sake, and spend the remaining life in solitude." 

A messenger is a warrior appointed on a front:
This assumption is not a sin in itself, but a messenger, as I have pointed out, holds the status of a warrior appointed on a front by Allah; therefore, it is not permissible for him to leave the field by his own opinion, even if the motive for this leaving is the passion of zeal for truth. If he does so, implicitly in it is hidden: he can protect himself from God's trial. This implicit aspect was not clear to Hazrat Yunus; therefore, Allah opened it so that its severity might come before his eyes. It should be kept in mind that sometimes a man takes an action which is completely innocent, but it reflects an assumption which is wrong, and attention does not go to it until some wise person draws attention to it. Allah here unveiled this very assumption of Hazrat Yunus so that its reform might take place. In the matter of inner reform and training, this is the wise method. The prophets (peace be upon them) are models for the entire humanity; therefore, if any minor slip is committed by them, the Wise and All-Knowing Lord shows them, through His special X-ray, where their action could lead if not addressed.

If this interpretation of this segment is taken, it is completely in accordance with intellect and nature, no blame is cast on Hazrat Yunus by it, and there remains no need for that artificiality which people have done in the interpretation of "lan naqdira 'alayhi" by deviating from the language. Yet, even after that artificiality, they could not solve the difficulty present in it.

The prayer of Hazrat Yunus:
"Fanada fee adh-dhulumati an la ilaha illa anta subhanaka innee kuntu mina adh-dhalimeen"—here, the details of the event Hazrat Yunus faced in the ship are omitted. Only that prayer is mentioned which he made inside the darkness of the fish's belly and the depth of the sea, which Allah accepted. The reason for this omission, as we have pointed out above, is that the purpose here is not the detail of the story, but only to show that the Merciful Lord accepts the prayers of His servant even inside the darkest situations, and saves him from the greatest calamity, provided the servant considers his Lord alone as the refuge and makes prayer before Him.

In the Book of Jonah, the mention of this prayer has come in these words:
"Then Jonah prayed unto the Lord his God out of the fish's belly, and said, I cried by reason of mine affliction unto the Lord, and he heard me; out of the belly of hell cried I, and thou heardest my voice." Book of Jonah 2:1-2

Meaning of darkness:
The word "dhulumat" (darkness) expresses the intensity of this darkness, the width of its surroundings, and its boundlessness. In the Arabic language, plural sometimes comes in view of the width of the surroundings of a thing. We have given reference to "mushariq wa magharib" in its example. Here, it refers to the darkness of the fish's belly and the depths of the sea. The purpose of this word is to clarify: let no one remain in the misunderstanding that to let God hear one's word, some public platform or radio and television are needed. He hears the cry of His servants even from the darkness of the depths and the depths of the sea, and does their help.

Hazrat Yunus's confession of fault:
"la ilaha illa anta subhanaka innee kuntu mina adh-dhalimeen"—this is the prayer of Hazrat Yunus. In this prayer, there is no demand or desire for anything from him; only the confession and expression of his fault is there. After the confession of fault, he left his matter to his Lord: that whatever He will decide is truth, and in that is wisdom and mercy, because He is pure from every defect. First, the expression of God alone being the Deity and refuge is done, after that, He is declared pure and sanctified from every defect, then the confession of his fault is made: "This which has presented itself is entirely the result of my own mistake. I myself wronged my own soul; my Lord did not do any wrong to me."

Although Hazrat Yunus did not bring the demand on his tongue and only cast himself before his Lord, the Lord, Knower of the Unseen, who hears the cries of the depths and knows the secrets of the hearts, removed all his grief and pain. By grief and pain here is meant not only that grief and pain which was faced due to the ship's incident, but also that grief and pain due to which he had abandoned his nation. In another place in the Quran, it is specified that after this incident, when he called his nation anew, his entire nation believed, and God saved them from the punishment hovering over their heads.

A segment to make the story correspond to the situation:
"Wa kadhalika nunjee al-mu'mineen" (And thus We save the believers)—this segment is to make the story correspond to the situation. It is obvious that the purpose of narrating these events is not storytelling, but to assure the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions: "No matter how dark and hopeless the situations may be, do not be disheartened. Allah knows and hears your prayers and cries. He saves His believing servants from every grief and pain in this manner, as He saved Jonah. A day will come when all these darknesses in which you are surrounded will disappear, truth will prevail, and your nation will be illuminated with the light of faith."

Acceptance of Hazrat Zakariyya's prayer:
The life story of Hazrat Zakariyya (Zechariah) has passed in detail in Surah Maryam. He too made prayer to his Lord in highly hopeless situations, but Allah made the impossible possible for him against all apparent situations. In the commentary of Surah Maryam, it has passed: his wife was barren, and he himself had reached the final stage of old age. Relatives were all incapable and indifferent to religion. Hazrat Zakariyya, in his old age, became worried: "The religious heritage of the family of Jacob, of which he is the heir, no one capable of it has been born in the family, and his own end has now arrived." In this worry, he prayed: "O Lord, do not leave me alone, but grant some heir who may become the carrier of my and the family of Jacob's religious traditions."

"Wa anta khayru al-waritheen"—meaning, this prayer I am making for an heir is not because if I have no heir, there will remain no carrier of Your religion. No work of Yours is bound with me or my family; You are the best heir of Your religion yourself. The meaning is: I am making this prayer only so that the family may not be deprived of the success of serving Your religion, which it has possessed by Your favor. Otherwise, I know that You will raise whomsoever You will for this service; You are not in need of anyone.

Hazrat Zakariyya's prayer was for a high religious purpose:
Hazrat Zakariyya's prayer was for a highly exalted religious purpose. He did not desire children but a servant of religion; the heritage of which he was the carrier was not the heritage of wealth and property but the heritage of the religion of the family of Jacob, and he wanted this sacred trust to remain protected; therefore, his prayer was worthy of acceptance. Allah made his barren wife capable of children, and granted him a renowned son like Hazrat Yahya (John).

"Innahum kanoo yusari'oona fee al-khayrati wa yad'oonana raghaban wa rahaban wa kanoo lana khashi'een"—it is stated: "In completely unfavorable and unhelpful situations, this prayer of theirs was worthy of acceptance because these people were not those who ran after the world, but were those who took precedence in the path of goodness and righteousness." In "Innahum", the plural pronoun is for the specific household of Hazrat Zakariyya. His cousins, as is clear from Surah Maryam, were people indifferent to religion, but those associated with Hazrat Zakariyya specifically were religious. Among these people, the disease of worldly competition had not been born; rather, that spirit of taking precedence in goodness was alive which was the prominent characteristic of the family of Jacob. By the birth of Hazrat Yahya, this spirit obtained a fresh body.

Their second attribute is stated: "yad'oonana raghaban wa rahaban"—meaning, they called upon their Lord in every state of hope and fear. In the Quran, the state of worldly people and polytheists is described: "When some difficulty presents itself, they make very long prayers to Us and make covenants that if their need is fulfilled, they will always remain loyal servants of God; but when God fulfills their need, they sing the praises of their own planning and others' helping, and forget God as if they had never called Him." It is stated: "The character of these people was not hypocritical or polytheistic, but they called upon God in every state of greed and fear, hope and terror."

"Wa kanoo lana khashi'een"—meaning, it was not that if they obtained a blessing, they would become arrogant, and if it were snatched, they would become hopeless and ungrateful; rather, in every state they bowed down before God.

This is a pointer to Hazrat Maryam (Mary), whose mention has passed in detail in Surah Maryam. Here, her name is not taken because the attribute mentioned of her is such that its subject and referent cannot be anyone other than her. This is proof of her specificity with this attribute.

Meaning of "ahsanat farjaha":
Do not take "ahsanat farjaha" in its common literal meaning. "Ahsana farjahu" is an idiom in Arabic, just as "badda ath-thulmata", "raqa al-fatqa", "jabara al-kasra", etc., are idioms. In these idioms, the literal meaning of words is not considered, but that meaning is considered for which they are used. The meaning of "ahsanat farjaha" is: "she kept herself pure from every evil or protected from danger." This idiom is used for women just as it is used for men. The word "farj" in Arabic means principally "mowdi'u makhafatin" (place of fear/vulnerability). I can present its testimonies, but only those can benefit from them whose sight is on the speech of Arabic. Common readers of the book will not benefit from them.

Meaning of Hazrat Isa being a spirit:
Since the conception of Hazrat Isa (Jesus) in the mother's womb occurred apart from the common law of the world of means, through the command "Kun" (Be) alone, Allah has expressed him as a spirit cast by Him. On this basis, he and his mother are also established as a great sign among the signs of Allah. We have explained the aspects of their being a sign in the commentary of Surah Maryam. Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him), as is clear from the Gospels, himself said regarding his relation what the Quran is stating; this is merely the effort of Paul's follower Christians who tried to make a mountain out of a molehill through their alterations. We have discussed these alterations in detail in the commentary of Al-Imran.

Summary of the discussion of prophets' stories:
After mentioning all the prophets, now as a summary of the discussion, it is stated: "The call and religion of all of them from the beginning to the end is one, and all these prophets are one single nation and community, and I am your Lord." The meaning is: if anyone has made division among the prophets, invented innovations in the religion brought by them, and mixed Shirk in the monotheism of God, the responsibility for it lies upon himself. The mantle of these prophets is pure from these things.

Threat to those creating division in God's religion:
This is a threat to those who, after creating innovations in this religion sent by God, broke it into pieces, and now every group in support of its own way is opposing this common religion brought by the prophets, which is being presented before them through the Quran and the final messenger of God. It is stated: "Do not care about these opponents; the return of all of them is to Us alone, no other place of return is to be; We will place before everyone their record. Nothing of anyone will remain hidden."

Meaning: "Since the return of all will be to Us alone, those who will do righteous deeds, on the condition that they are firm on faith and monotheism, should remain assured that their effort will not go in vain. We will give them a full reward. We will keep every single good deed of theirs recorded. There is no fear that any action of theirs will remain out of Our record." Only this is the condition: it must have been done with faith. We have explained this reality repeatedly: in God's presence, only that action is acceptable which is done with faith, solely for the sake of the One God. An action not done for God does not possess the value of a penny in God's presence, even if it be migration and Jihad.

The upcoming theme... Verses 95-112:
Forward are the concluding verses of the Surah. The same theme of warning with which the Surah began is reminded anew at the end in a new style, and the introduction to this conclusion begins with the exact same word with which the Surah began. At the beginning, He said: "Iqtariaba linnasi hisabuhum" (Draws near for the people their reckoning). Here, when the same theme is taken anew, He said: "Waqtaraba al-wa'du al-haqqu" (And draws near the true promise). In this manner, the Quran itself guides to its order. In most surahs, this style is present. The teacher-imam expresses this style as "returning to the beginning" (al-'awd 'ala al-bad'). Its benefit is: in the spread of the Surah's demands, if the actual purpose becomes out of sight, it is refreshed anew in the listener's mind. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

A style of omission, and the decisive verb in the meaning of past action:
In this verse, in accordance with the well-known style of Arabic, a part is omitted, whose explanation is done by the sentence "annahum la yarji'oon". If you open the entire matter, it will be: "Wa haramun 'ala qaryatin ahlaknaha an yarji'oo annahum la yarji'oon" (And forbidden it is for a town We destroyed to return—that they will not return). Many examples of this kind of omission have passed in this book, and in it, the verb "ahlaknaha" (We destroyed) is in the meaning of the decision of the verb. Sometimes, when the cut-off nature of a matter is intended to be expressed, it is stated in such a manner as if it has already occurred.

Direction to turn away from the wicked of Quraysh:
In this verse, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is stopped from pursuing the wicked of Quraysh too much: "These people have now come under the target of the Divine Sunnah. Just as the past nations who were destroyed, despite all efforts, did not believe, similarly now, entering into faith has become forbidden for these people, and because of their obstinacy, the divine punishment has been decreed for them." The meaning is: "Now do not grieve much over these people; refer their matter to Allah." This same theme is stated in verse 6 of this Surah:
(Not a town believed before them which We destroyed; so will they believe?)

Until when Gog and Magog are opened and they slide down from every elevation.

No nation before them which fell under the target of destruction became believing—so do you expect from these that they will believe!

The dominance of Gog and Magog is among the signs of Resurrection:
On Gog and Magog, a detailed discussion has passed under verses 98-99 of Surah Al-Kahf. It is known from the Holy Quran that at the time of the manifestation of Resurrection, Gog and Magog will pour down from every side, and the entire world will be filled with corruption. What form will this take? This thing is among the allegorical matters (mutashabihat), and the true reality of allegorical matters is in the knowledge of Allah alone. Here, the purpose of its mention is only to show: "Those who today are demanding various kinds of signs and observation of punishment to believe in the Quran—behind them now, the Prophet should not waste his time. These people are not to be tamed by these arguments of intellect and nature which the Quran is presenting before them; rather, they will believe only when the Resurrection stands on their heads, and at that time, believing or not believing will be equal."

The Resurrection stands on the head:
That is, this promise of Resurrection which they are considering a far-off matter is not far-off but the time of its manifestation has drawn very near. In Surah Al-Ma'arij, He said: (Indeed, they see it as distant, but We see it as near.) [Al-Ma'arij: 6-7]. Man measures everything with his small scales; therefore, Resurrection seems very far-off to him, whereas in terms of divine days, it stands right on the head. This same theme has passed in the first verse of this Surah too. There we have explained some of its other aspects too; take a look at them too.

"Fa-idha hiya shakhiatun absaru alladhina kafaroo"—meaning, it will come suddenly, and its scene will be so ہولناک that those who today demand it with defiance and mock it—their eyes will remain fixed. This is the picture of fear and terror. When man suddenly sees some ہولناک thing, he remains looking at it, stunned.

The true veil in the matter of Resurrection:
"Ya waylana qad kunna fee ghaflatin min hadha bal kunna dhalimeen"—this is the expression of their state at that time; therefore, "qaloo" (they said) is omitted here. It is stated: "They themselves will cry out at that time: 'Indeed, we remained in heedlessness of an impending reality.' Bal kunna dhalimeen—meaning, it is not that no warner came to awaken us from this heedlessness. Allah sent warners too who exerted all their strength to awaken us, but we ourselves became wrongdoers to our own souls that we mocked their warnings." It should be kept in mind that the true veil in the matter of Resurrection is not that there is no convincing argument for it; rather, the true veil is man's heedlessness. He does not want to believe in a reality, despite the clarity of its arguments, because to believe in it is against his desires. In such a state, the only thing that supports him is his heedlessness. He remains in heedlessness, postponing any preparation for it, until one day it arrives on his head.

Idols and their worshippers both in Hell:
That is, at that time, this confession of theirs will not be of any benefit to them; rather, the command from Allah will be: "You and all these idols and stones whom you worshipped besides God will all become the fuel of Hell." "Antum laha waridoon"—meaning, now cry or wail, you have to inevitably descend to this landing-place of destruction. Now there is no escape from it.

In "ma ta'budoona" (what you worship), "ma" is a clue that here it refers to those idols and stones whom the pagan Arabs worshipped. The use of "ma" is well-known for inanimate things. Making them the fuel of Hell, as is obvious, is not to punish these stones, but to show their worshippers that those whom they considered deities and bowed down to—what is their state here. The angels or jinn whom the pagan Arabs worshipped—such as Lat, Uzza, Manat, and Hubal, etc.—first, these were their own assumed imaginary names whose referent did not exist at all; then, their worship too they did only in the form of stone statues. Some polytheistic nations who worship prophets and righteous people—their matters are not under discussion here. The details of their matters have come in other places in the Quran. For example, Christians worship Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) and Hazrat Maryam. Against them, the testimony Hazrat Isa will give on the Day of Resurrection, its details have passed in Surah Al-Ma'idah. Similarly, other prophets and righteous people too will declare detachment from their worshippers on the Day of Resurrection: "What do we know that you worshipped us! If you committed this foolishness, now suffer its punishment! When did we tell you to worship us!"

The purpose of putting the idols in Hell:
This is the actual purpose for which these idols and stones will be thrown into Hell. We have pointed out above: throwing them into Hell is to show their worshippers that if they were deities, they would not have met this end! This will be the final limit of their humiliation: those who were the final refuge of all their hopes, they will find them helpless, forced, and arrested in punishment like themselves. "Wa kullun feeha khalidoon"—the first part of this statement will be said addressing them, and the second part is the proclamation of their eternal end.

If there is no confusion, the dispersion of pronouns is not a defect, but brevity:
"Zafeer" means sighing, crying, and wailing, and the pronoun "Hum" in the first sentence, in my view, is for the worshippers, and in the second sentence, for their idols and stones. Although in this form, there is apparently a dispersion of pronouns, yet the dispersion of pronouns, as we have explained in places through examples, is a defect only when there is fear of confusion in the referent. If this fear is not there, and a clear clue is present for the determination of the referent, then it is not a defect but is the beauty of speech in terms of brevity (Ijaz). Here, there is no fear of confusion. The meaning of the verse is: the worshippers will be crying, wailing, and sighing in Hell, and those solid stones whom they worshipped as deities will not even know that someone is crying and wailing and seeking help from them. This theme is stated in other places in the Quran too in different styles: that the people of Hell will cry for help, but there will be no one to help them. It is possible that someone may have a doubt: in the second segment, the pronoun and verb used are those used for intelligent beings—how then is their use appropriate for idols and stones? The answer to this is: this pronoun and verb are used according to the assumption of the worshippers. Since they considered these stones as their helper and deity, this style of expression was adopted. Examples of this in the speech of Arabic and the Holy Quran are many.

Glad tidings for those of good end:
The subject of "husna" is omitted here. The meaning is: those for whom the promise of a good end has already been made from Us, those people indeed will be kept far from this Hell. The promise of this good end has passed in verse 94. There He said: those who do righteous deeds while remaining firm on faith, Allah will not disregard their efforts; rather, He has kept their every action recorded, and they will receive a full reward for their every action.

Refutation of a wrong notion:
That is, they will be kept so far from this Hell that they will not even hear its sound. From this verse, the notion of those who, due to not determining the address of verse 71 of Surah Maryam "Wa in minkum illa wariduha" correctly, fell into the misunderstanding that, God forbid, all believers too must pass through Hell once, is being refuted in highly clear words. We have determined the address of the mentioned verse, and in the light of the verse under discussion, refuting this common notion of commentators, clarified the mutual compatibility of both verses. Take a look at it.

Glad tidings of eternal kingdom:
By "faza'un akbar" (the greatest terror) is meant that great commotion and worry which will be established in the entire universe after the blowing of the trumpet, whose ہولناک picture the Quran has drawn in places. Particularly in the surahs of the last group, the Quran's miracle of expression has مصور it in such a manner that if man possesses a seeing eye, he sees it with his eyes. Above, verse 97 points to this by "Fa-idha hiya shakhiatun absaru alladhina kafaroo". It is stated: "This day of great commotion will not cause the slightest anxiety to Our believing servants; rather, on that day, Our angels will welcome them with slogans of welcome and glad tidings: 'This is your day which you were promised through the prophets and messengers. Now, congratulations to you on the eternal kingdom!'"

Meaning of Sijill and Kutub:
"Sijill" refers to that register, scroll, or file in which written papers are rolled. "Kutub" here is in the meaning of written papers. "Awwala khalqin" is in the place of a vessel. To express this same theme, in some places in the Quran, the word "awwala marratin" (the first time) has also entered.

New heaven and new earth:
The meaning is: all this will happen on that day when We will roll up the carpet of this spread heaven in the manner papers are rolled up in a scroll; and just as We created the heaven and earth the first time, similarly We will create them anew with new laws and principles.

In "wa'dan 'alayna" (a promise upon Us), the verbal noun is for emphasis—meaning, this is a binding promise upon Us. We have made a cut-off promise with Our righteous servants, as is pointed out in "kuntum too'adoon" of the above verse, whose fulfillment is Our responsibility. "Inna kunna fa'ileen"—meaning, let no one think of these as merely imaginary statements which We will not be able to do or will not do. We will surely do this, We have decided it from before, and this is a highly easy matter for Us.

The heirs of the earth will be the righteous:
In this verse, reference is made to the Psalms (Zabur). In the Psalms, this matter is stated in places that the heirs of the earth will be the righteous servants of God, but Chapter 37 is entirely specified for the explanation of this single reality; therefore, we transfer this entire chapter here so that the correctness and truth of the Quran's reference may become clear, and it also becomes known what earth's inheritance is meant here, as well as the correct meaning of "min ba'di adh-dhikri" as to what Dhikr points to. First, we transfer the Psalm of Hazrat Dawud, after that, we will point to those matters which are particularly worthy of attention for the explanation of the verse under discussion:

"Fret not thyself because of evildoers,
neither be thou envious against the workers of iniquity.
For they shall soon be cut down like the grass,
and wither as the green herb.
Trust in the Lord, and do good;
so shalt thou dwell in the land, and verily thou shalt be fed.
Delight thyself also in the Lord;
and he shall give thee the desires of thine heart.
Commit thy way unto the Lord;
trust also in him; and he shall bring it to pass.
And he shall bring forth thy righteousness as the light,
and thy judgment as the noonday.
Rest in the Lord, and wait patiently for him:
fret not thyself because of him who prospereth in his way,
because of the man who bringeth wicked devices to pass.
Cease from anger, and forsake wrath:
fret not thyself in any wise to do evil.
For evildoers shall be cut off:
but those that wait upon the Lord, they shall inherit the earth.
For yet a little while, and the wicked shall not be:
yea, thou shalt diligently consider his place, and it shall not be.
But the meek shall inherit the earth;
and shall delight themselves in the abundance of peace.
The wicked plotteth against the just,
and gnasheth upon him with his teeth.
The Lord shall laugh at him:
for he seeth that his day is coming.
The wicked have drawn out the sword, and have bent their bow,
to cast down the poor and needy,
and to slay such as be of upright conversation.
Their sword shall enter into their own heart,
and their bows shall be broken.
A little that a righteous man hath
is better than the riches of many wicked.
For the arms of the wicked shall be broken:
but the Lord upholdeth the righteous.
The Lord knoweth the days of the upright:
and their inheritance shall be forever.
They shall not be ashamed in the evil time:
and in the days of famine they shall be satisfied.
But the wicked shall perish,
and the enemies of the Lord shall be as the fat of lambs:
they shall consume; into smoke shall they consume away.
The wicked borroweth, and payeth not again:
but the righteous showeth mercy, and giveth.
For such as be blessed of him shall inherit the earth;
and they that be cursed of him shall be cut off.
The steps of a good man are ordered by the Lord:
and he delighteth in his way.
Though he fall, he shall not be utterly cast down:
for the Lord upholdeth him with his hand.
I have been young, and now am old;
yet have I not seen the righteous forsaken,
nor his seed begging bread.
He is ever merciful, and lendeth;
and his seed is blessed.
Depart from evil, and do good;
and dwell for evermore.
For the Lord loveth judgment,
and forsaketh not his saints;
they are preserved for ever:
but the seed of the wicked shall be cut off.
The righteous shall inherit the land,
and dwell therein for ever.
The mouth of the righteous speaketh wisdom,
and his tongue talketh of judgment.
The law of his God is in his heart;
none of his steps shall slide.
The wicked watcheth the righteous,
and seeketh to slay him.
The Lord will not leave him in his hand,
