nor condemn him when he is judged.
Wait on the Lord, and keep his way,
and he shall exalt thee to inherit the land..."

Some prominent aspects of this Psalm:
In our view, the Quran has referenced this very Psalm here. If you ponder over this Psalm, it will be clearly seen that its nature is like a compound poem in which first the matters of advice and exhortation come, then repeatedly like a refrain, this matter comes that the heirs of the earth and land will be the righteous and God-fearing servants of God. This style of speech is similar to the style of Surah Ar-Rahman of the Quran in which the refrain is "Fabi-ayyi ala'i rabbikuma tukadhdhiban". If you cast a look at the underlined sentences, this matter will also be known: along with the inheritance of the earth for the righteous and God-fearing, this glad tiding is also there that this inheritance will be eternal. For example, it is said: "their inheritance shall be forever"; in another place, "dwell for evermore"; in a third place, "they are preserved for ever"; in a fourth place in highly clear words, "The righteous shall inherit the land, and dwell therein for ever." [1] It is obvious that this eternal inheritance is not related to this earth on which we are populated. Neither is any of its things eternal, nor is its inheritance specified for the righteous; rather, this earth and its every thing is fani, and in it, Allah has granted respite to both the good and the bad for a period, after which both heaven and earth will perish, and with new laws and principles, a new world will be born whose eternal inheritance will be obtained by the righteous, and the people of falsehood will be thrown into Hell.

[1] See the underlined sentences inside the Psalm.

This exact same matter is proved from the Quran too. In the above verse 104, it is clearly specified that Allah will roll up the carpet of this heaven one day. In Surah Ibrahim, verse 48, regarding both heaven and earth, this specification is present: (On the day when the earth will be replaced with another earth, and the heavens...) [Ibrahim: 48]. Inside this replaced heaven and earth, undoubtedly this law will be: their eternal inheritance and kingdom will be obtained only by the righteous. For the disobedient, there will be no portion in it. This matter is proved not only from the Psalms and the Quran, but from all the divine scrolls and the teachings of all the prophets and messengers.

This very reality has been clarified by the Quran in the verse under discussion through the reference to the Psalms. The Psalms are the collection of divine songs sent down to Hazrat Dawud (peace be upon him). Hazrat Dawud, as is stated in this Surah, was a prophet and also a majestic king of this earth. From the tongue of a king, this proclamation could be highly appropriate: the true and final heirs of the earth will be only the righteous servants of God. When a majestic king has proclaimed this reality, where is the room for anyone else to speak.

Review of the parts of the verse:
After this explanation, now ponder over the parts of the verse under discussion.

"Wa laqad katabna fee az-zaboori min ba'di adh-dhikri"—above, through the reference to Chapter 37 of the Psalms, this matter has become clear that the Quran has pointed here to that teaching of the Psalms which is stated in detail, in a highly effective manner, in an independent chapter, and this matter has also become clear: the style of expression adopted is that the actual matter is said with refrain and repetition, and every time, before the actual matter, the highly exalted matters of wisdom and advice are urged. To this very thing, the Quran has pointed by "min ba'di adh-dhikri" (after the reminder). The importance of this pointer is: in fact, these are the advices, practicing which is the guarantor of the eternal kingdom of the earth.

"Anna al-arda yarithuha 'ibadiya as-salihood"—this is the actual matter for which the Quran has referenced the Psalms. From the references of the Psalms, this matter is proved: here, by "al-ard" (the earth) is meant the land of that new world which will come into existence after the Resurrection, and whose owner and heir, without partnership of others, will be only the righteous servants of God.

The word "balagh" (proclamation) here contains the meaning of both public proclamation and general glad tiding, and the indefinite form of the word is for highlighting its status. The meaning is: this decision of God that the heirs of the earth will be only the righteous servants of God is a great proclamation of glad tiding for His worshipping servants—so whoever wants to play the game, let him play it for this, why should he ruin his eternal kingdom behind this short life!

Meaning: "For this very warning and glad tiding, We have sent you as a mercy for the worlds, to inform them of this reality. Upon you is only the duty of proclamation and warning, so perform this duty. If the arrogant insolent people mock your warning and demand the showing of that promised time, say to them: 'God has sent me as a mercy, not as a punishment. I do not know when the punishment will come. Its time is with my Lord alone.'"

Warn them: "What has been revealed to me is: your Deity is only One Deity, no other is His partner; so do not ruin your Hereafter on the trust of anyone else. No other will save you from God's grasp."

In "fahal antum muslimoon" (will you then submit?), there is a highly powerful warning, asking: "Tell, are you ready to submit yourselves to this one powerful Lord or not?" The meaning is: "My duty was to warn, which I have performed; now the responsibility is yours. If you persist in this obstinacy of yours and keep mocking my word on the strength of your partners and intercessors, I am free of responsibility. Now the responsibility for the results is upon you."

The decisive warning:
That is, if even after this decisive warning of yours, they remain firm on their turning away, say to them: "I have informed your big and small, leader and follower, rich and poor, without distinction, of the impending danger. Now no one among you can make the excuse that he was not warned. The correct knowledge of it is only with that God whose command I am informing you of."

Indeed, He knows what is declared of speech, and He knows what you conceal.

Entrusting matters to Allah:
This is a highly comprehensive and eloquent statement. The meaning is: God knows your open speech and also knows what you conceal in your chests; so the matter is referred to Him alone who will do what His wisdom demands. He knows what you are demanding from me and what is hidden in the depth of this demand of yours. It should be kept in mind that those who demanded the punishment from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) did not do so because in fact they considered the punishment of Resurrection impossible or the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a liar, but they did so only to corner you and deceive their common folk. Pointing to this, He said: "Do not think that God is unaware of the secrets of your hearts. Everything is clear to Him; therefore, I do not need to be worried by this demand of yours. I have full trust in His knowledge and His wisdom."

In "la'allahu" (perhaps it), the pronoun refers to that delay of the promise pointed to in the above verse. That is: "This promise of punishment which is being delayed—I cannot say what secret is in this delay. God alone knows this secret. Perhaps it is a trial for you, so that the proof upon you may be fully completed and your cup fully filled, and perhaps Allah wants to grant you some more time to enjoy." In any case, whatever the cause, instead of being arrogant on this respite, try to benefit from it. If the first form is there, make haste for repentance, and if the second form is there, adopt the path of gratitude.

Prayer and farewell:
This is the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) prayer and also farewell to the opponents.

To Allah, you made this prayer: "O Lord, now decide this struggle with truth," and you bid farewell to the opponents in this manner: "Against what you say and do, I seek help from my Merciful Lord alone, because the True Helper is He alone."

On these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise be to Allah for this.
